problem_statement.Title: Pretty-printer duplicates basis vector name and misaligns baseline for vectors with multiline scalar coefficients
problem_statement.Description: When pretty-printing BasisDependent (vector) expressions whose scalar coefficients render as multi-line prettyForms (for example (x/y)**t), the terminal/pretty output produced by sympy.printing.pretty.PrettyPrinter shows the basis vector name duplicated on multiple lines and the basis name vertically misaligned relative to the scalar block.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py:PrettyPrinter._print_BasisDependent — Incorrect assumption and fragile implementation: the method converts a coefficient's prettyForm into a raw string and then repositions the basis vector name via ad-hoc substring/newline manipulation. Evidence in the method: it calls self._print(v).parens()[0] to obtain a string for the coefficient and then concatenates k._pretty_form into that string (e.g. arg_str + ' ' + k._pretty_form). It later attempts to repair multiline output by removing and reinserting vectstrs (tempstr.replace(vectstrs[i], '') then replacing RIGHT PARENTHESIS UPPER HOOK + vectstr) and by performing manual newline/length arithmetic using hard-coded spacing (numeric 3). These string-level operations are brittle for multiline prettyForms and lead to repeated basis names and incorrect vertical centering.

2) sympy/vector/vector.py:BaseVector and sympy/vector/coordsysrect.py:CoordSys3D — Representation assumption: BaseVector instances store their human-readable basis-name as a plain string in the attribute _pretty_form (created in CoordSys3D and assigned in BaseVector.__new__ via obj._pretty_form = u'' + pretty_str). PrettyPrinter._print_BasisDependent relies on inserting those raw basis-name strings into stringified coefficient output rather than composing prettyForm objects. This mismatch (some objects' pretty output being constructed/handled as strings while the pretty-printer builds and composes prettyForm objects) underlies the fragility.

3) sympy/vector/dyadic.py:BaseDyadic — Secondary finding with the same root-cause pattern: BaseDyadic._pretty_form is constructed by concatenating BaseVector._pretty_form strings (obj._pretty_form = '(' + vector1._pretty_form + '|' + vector2._pretty_form + ')'), i.e. building pretty representations as plain strings rather than prettyForm objects. This is the same representation pattern that contributes to fragility when pretty output is later manipulated as strings.
StepsToReproduce: Observe pretty-printed output in an interactive session/console
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The scalar coefficient ((x/y)**t) is rendered as a single multiline pretty block (parenthesized/stacked) and the basis vector name (e_j) appears exactly once to the right of that block, vertically centered relative to the coefficient block. Unit coefficients are displayed without an explicit '1' (e.g. e_j) and -1 is displayed as '(-1) e_j', both with correct alignment.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The pretty output shows the basis vector name repeated on multiple lines of the multiline scalar (one copy per line fragment) and/or the basis name is vertically misaligned (baseline not centered). Example: the vector name 'e_j' appears on every line of the coefficient's multiline pretty representation instead of once to the right and centered.

problem_statement.Title: LaTeX printer for Poly does not preserve Poly monomial ordering (differs from str/pretty)
problem_statement.Description: When rendering a Poly, the LaTeX printer emits the polynomial's terms in an order that differs from the str() and pretty() printers. This manifests as monomials appearing in a non-logical sequence (not highest-to-lowest degree) in the LaTeX output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/latex.py : LatexPrinter._print_Poly — this method converts the Poly to a SymPy Expr and prints that Expr; specifically it calls self._print(poly.as_expr()). (latex.py: _print_Poly: expr = self._print(poly.as_expr())). Because it prints the Expr form produced by Poly.as_expr(), the LaTeX output's term order depends on how that Expr (an Add) was constructed and on the Add/printing path, not on Poly.terms().

2) sympy/polys/polytools.py : Poly.as_expr — Poly.as_expr constructs an Expr by calling basic_from_dict(f.rep.to_sympy_dict(), *gens). (polytools.py: as_expr: return basic_from_dict(f.rep.to_sympy_dict(), *gens)). This converts the internal polynomial representation into a mapping/dict-based form and delegates the reconstruction of an Add to the helper in polyutils.

3) sympy/polys/polyutils.py : basic_from_dict / expr_from_dict — expr_from_dict builds an Add by iterating rep.items(): for monom, coeff in rep.items(): ... result.append(Mul(*term)); return Add(*result). There is no step in expr_from_dict that sorts monomials according to Poly's lex ordering, so the order of Add's args follows the dict iteration order of rep.to_sympy_dict(). (polyutils.py: expr_from_dict uses rep.items() and returns Add(*result)).

4) sympy/polys/polytools.py : Poly.terms — Poly exposes terms() (and monoms(), coeffs()) whose docstrings state they return non-zero terms/monomials in lex order and whose implementation delegates to f.rep.terms(order=order). (polytools.py: def terms(f, order=None): "Returns all non-zero terms from ``f`` in lex order."; implementation: return [(m, f.rep.dom.to_sympy(c)) for m, c in f.rep.terms(order=order)]). The str/pretty printers rely on this API to produce the canonical (highest-to-lowest degree) ordering.

5) sympy/printing/str.py and sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py : their Poly printing implementations iterate expr.terms() (str.py: for monom, coeff in expr.terms(): ...) and/or otherwise print Add expressions using code paths that request lex ordering in places. Because they iterate Poly.terms(), they render monomials in the intended lex (highest-to-lowest) order while the LaTeX route prints the Expr created by as_expr() (which was built from a dict) and therefore can show a different sequence of terms.
StepsToReproduce: Poly(a*x**5 + x**4 + b*x**3 + 2*x**2 + c*x + 3, x, domain='ZZ[a,b,c]')
Poly(a*x**5 + x**4 + b*x**3 + 2*x**2 + c*x + 3, x, domain='ZZ[a,b,c]')
\\operatorname{Poly}{\\left( a x^{5} + b x^{3} + c x + x^{4} + 2 x^{2} + 3, x, domain=\\mathbb{Z}\\left[a, b, c\\right] \\right)}
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: LaTeX rendering of a Poly should show monomials in the same logical order as str() and pretty(): highest-to-lowest degree according to the Poly monomial ordering (e.g. a*x**5 + x**4 + b*x**3 + 2*x**2 + c*x + 3).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: LaTeX rendering shows terms in a different sequence than str()/pretty(). For the repro above, latex(p) outputs a polynomial with terms ordered as: a x^5 + b x^3 + c x + x^4 + 2 x^2 + 3 (i.e., x^4 appears after x^3), which differs from the Poly/str/pretty highest-to-lowest ordering.

problem_statement.Title: Poly(domain='RR[y,z]') raises OptionError: Domain string parsing rejects RR as polynomial ground
problem_statement.Description: Passing a composite domain string like 'RR[y,z]' to Poly(..., domain=...) raises OptionError in Domain.preprocess even though the codebase itself produces such strings (PolynomialRing.__str__ yields strings like 'RR[y,z]'). The parser for domain strings in sympy/polys/polyoptions.py does not accept composite domain strings whose ground is RR (or other non-integer/non-rational grounds), and therefore rejects valid-looking domain strings.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Overly-restrictive regexes in sympy/polys/polyoptions.py::Domain: the class-level patterns that detect polynomial and fraction composite domain strings are hardcoded to accept only Z/ZZ/Q/QQ as the ground. The current definitions are exactly:
   _re_polynomial = re.compile(r"^(Z|ZZ|Q|QQ)\[(.+)\]$")
   _re_fraction = re.compile(r"^(Z|ZZ|Q|QQ)\((.+)\)$")
   Because of these patterns, strings like 'RR[y,z]' (ground 'RR') do not match the polynomial/fraction patterns and are not handled by those branches.

2) Non-recursive ground handling and simplistic fallback: when the polynomial/fraction pattern is matched, Domain.preprocess only distinguishes 'Z'/'ZZ' (uses ZZ.poly_ring/ZZ.frac_field) versus everything else (uses QQ.poly_ring/QQ.frac_field). Concretely, the code path for a matched polynomial pattern does:
   ground, gens = r.groups()
   gens = list(map(sympify, gens.split(',')))
   if ground in ['Z', 'ZZ']:
       return sympy.polys.domains.ZZ.poly_ring(*gens)
   else:
       return sympy.polys.domains.QQ.poly_ring(*gens)
   Thus the logic does not attempt to interpret the ground token as an arbitrary domain (for example, 'RR'), it reduces "non-Z" grounds to QQ.poly_ring. This is a second manifestation of the same incorrect assumption (that the only composite grounds are integer/rational domains).

3) Mismatch between stringification and parsing: sympy/polys/domains/polynomialring.py::PolynomialRing.__str__ constructs composite domain strings using the ground domain's string and the generator symbols as:
   return str(self.domain) + '[' + ','.join(map(str, self.symbols)) + ']'
   so the system prints domain objects such as 'RR[y,z]'. The parser in Domain.preprocess does not accept that string format when the ground is 'RR', causing a round-trip mismatch: the code can produce a string that the parser will reject.

4) Error message clarity: when Domain.preprocess finally rejects an input it raises the message exactly as implemented:
   raise OptionError('expected a valid domain specification, got %s' % domain)
   The message interpolates the domain without repr/quotes, which can make it slightly less clear in some contexts. This is an informational note about the current message text as observed in the code.
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python REPL with SymPy sources available.
From sympy import Poly, symbols; x, y, z = symbols('x y z')
Evaluate: Poly(1.2*x*y*z, x)  # observe default domain printed as 'RR[y,z]' in the resulting Poly's domain representation
Then evaluate: Poly(1.2*x*y*z, x, domain='RR[y,z]')  # use the same string as printed (domain='RR[y,z]')
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When given an explicit domain string that matches the stringification of a domain object (for example 'RR[y,z]'), Poly(..., domain=<that string>) should return a Poly instance with the corresponding composite domain (e.g. a PolynomialRing over RR with symbols y and z) and not raise an OptionError. In other words, domain strings produced by the library's own domain objects should be accepted when passed back as input strings.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling Poly(1.2*x*y*z, x, domain='RR[y,z]') raises an OptionError: "expected a valid domain specification, got RR[y,z]" (raised from sympy/polys/polyoptions.py::Domain.preprocess). The library prints a domain string like 'RR[y,z]' when creating a Poly without an explicit domain (Poly(1.2*x*y*z, x) shows domain 'RR[y,z]'), but passing that exact string explicitly to Poly(..., domain=...) is rejected by Domain.preprocess.

problem_statement.Title: LaTeX printer uses 'acsc'/'asec' instead of 'arccsc'/'arcsec' when inv_trig_style='full'
problem_statement.Description: When printing inverse cosecant (acsc) and inverse secant (asec) with the LaTeX printer and inv_trig_style="full", the printer emits 'acsc'/'asec' (e.g. "\\operatorname{acsc}{...}") instead of the expected full names 'arccsc'/'arcsec' (e.g. "\\operatorname{arccsc}{...}").
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/latex.py — LatexPrinter._print_Function: this method uses a hardcoded list
   inv_trig_table = ["asin", "acos", "atan", "acot"]
   to decide which inverse-trig function names receive special formatting when inv_trig_style is set. Because "acsc" and "asec" are omitted from this list, the branch that applies the "full" transformation (which constructs the full name via the pattern "arc" + func[1:] such that "acsc" -> "arccsc" and "asec" -> "arcsec") is not executed for them. Current hardcoded value: ["asin", "acos", "atan", "acot"]. The correct membership for the list to cover these functions is: ["asin", "acos", "atan", "acsc", "asec", "acot"].

2) sympy/physics/vector/printing.py — VectorLatexPrinter._print_Function: the same hardcoded list inv_trig_table = ["asin", "acos", "atan", "acot"] appears here and encodes the same incorrect assumption. This duplication means vector-specific LaTeX printing contexts exhibit the same omission for "acsc" and "asec". Current hardcoded value: ["asin", "acos", "atan", "acot"]. The correct membership for the list is: ["asin", "acos", "atan", "acsc", "asec", "acot"].
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python session with SymPy available.
from sympy import symbols, acsc, asec, latex
Optionally: print(latex(acsc(x), inv_trig_style='power')) and print(latex(asec(x), inv_trig_style='power')) to observe power-style output.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For inv_trig_style="full": latex(acsc(x), inv_trig_style="full") -> "\\operatorname{arccsc}{\\left (x \\right )}" and latex(asec(x), inv_trig_style="full") -> "\\operatorname{arcsec}{\\left (x \\right )}". For inv_trig_style="power": latex(acsc(x), inv_trig_style="power") -> "\\csc^{-1}{\\left (x \\right )}" and latex(asec(x), inv_trig_style="power") -> "\\sec^{-1}{\\left (x \\right )}".
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For inv_trig_style="full": latex(acsc(x), inv_trig_style="full") returns "\\operatorname{acsc}{\\left (x \\right )}" and latex(asec(x), inv_trig_style="full") returns "\\operatorname{asec}{\\left (x \\right )}" (the 'arc' prefix is not added). For inv_trig_style="power" the power-style outputs are unaffected (they produce "\\csc^{-1}..."/"\\sec^{-1}...").

problem_statement.Title: PrettyPrinter raises SympifyError when pretty-printing MatAdd containing MatrixSymbol with non-parseable name
problem_statement.Description: Pretty-printing a MatAdd (sum of matrix expressions) using the pretty printer can raise sympy.core.sympify.SympifyError when one of the summands is a MatrixSymbol whose name is not a parseable SymPy expression (for example, 'y*'). The pretty-printer calls sympify on an internal argument of the matrix expression, which can fail for such names and cause the exception shown in the traceback.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py — PrettyPrinter._print_MatAdd: this method decides whether to insert ' + ' or a single space before a summand by evaluating S(item.args[0]).is_negative (see pretty.py around lines 821–835). That invokes sympify(S(...)) on item.args[0]. For a MatrixSymbol summand, args[0] is the raw name object, and sympifying it can raise SympifyError when the name contains characters that are not a valid SymPy expression (e.g. 'y*').

2) sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py — MatrixSymbol.__new__ and .name: MatrixSymbol.__new__ constructs the object with the name passed unchanged (it sympifies only the shape arguments n and m). MatrixSymbol.name returns self.args[0], so args[0] may be an arbitrary Python object (commonly a string) and is not guaranteed to be a SymPy expression or parseable string. Thus code that calls S(item.args[0]) is relying on an unsafe assumption about the type/format of args[0].

3) Incorrect sign-detection assumption in PrettyPrinter._print_MatAdd: the printer attempts to infer negativity by sympifying a sub-argument (the name) and inspecting is_negative. In the matrix-expression API, a term's sign is a structural property of the expression (for product-form terms the scalar multiplier is represented separately from matrix factors). Concretely, MatMul provides APIs that expose the scalar coefficient (as_coeff_mmul / as_coeff_matrices) — the scalar coefficient, not the textual content of a matrix name, is the relevant object whose .is_negative indicates negativity. Relying on S(item.args[0]) to detect negativity is therefore both unnecessary and fragile because it attempts to parse raw names.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sympy import MatrixSymbol, pprint, symbols; n = symbols('n'); pprint(MatrixSymbol('x', n, n) + MatrixSymbol('y*', n, n))"
>>> from sympy import MatrixSymbol, pprint, symbols
>>> pprint(MatrixSymbol('x', n, n) + MatrixSymbol('y*', n, n))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The pretty printer returns a formatted (multi-line) string representation of the matrix sum and does not raise an exception. Both MatrixSymbol names (including names containing characters that are not parseable SymPy expressions, e.g. 'y*') appear in the output; negative summands are displayed with their leading '-' and positive summands are separated by ' + '.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling pprint / pretty on a MatAdd whose summand is MatrixSymbol('y*', n, n) raises sympy.core.sympify.SympifyError: "Sympify of expression 'could not parse 'y*'' failed". The traceback shows the error originates from sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py in _print_MatAdd when evaluating S(item.args[0]).is_negative.

problem_statement.Title: lambdify raises SyntaxError for MatrixSymbol names with braces even when dummify=True
problem_statement.Description: When a MatrixSymbol has a name containing characters that are not valid Python identifiers (for example "{v}"), calling lambdify on that MatrixSymbol can produce a SyntaxError. This occurs even when dummify=True because the lambdify code paths that decide which arguments to dummify and how to populate the exec namespace treat only core Symbol (and Function) instances as "symbol-like", excluding MatrixSymbol.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) _EvaluatorPrinter._preprocess only recognizes sympy.core.symbol.Symbol (and Function) as symbol-like for the purpose of dummification/sanitization and uses isinstance(arg, Symbol) to decide whether to create a Dummy and substitute it into the expression. Location: sympy/utilities/lambdify.py, class _EvaluatorPrinter, method _preprocess (see the branch: "elif isinstance(arg, Symbol):" which handles Symbol but not MatrixSymbol). Current check: isinstance(arg, Symbol). Correct relationship (diagnostic statement of what is missing): MatrixSymbol objects are symbol-like for lambdify's argument handling and must be considered in the same decision (i.e. treat instances of sympy.matrices.expressions.matexpr.MatrixSymbol like Symbol here).

2) lambdastr's inner sub_args duplicates the same type-checking logic and only replaces Function and Symbol instances with Dummy when dummify=True, excluding MatrixSymbol. Location: sympy/utilities/lambdify.py, function lambdastr -> inner sub_args (see "if isinstance(args, (Function, Symbol)):"). Current check: isinstance(args, (Function, Symbol)). Correct relationship: include MatrixSymbol in that tuple or otherwise treat MatrixSymbol as symbol-like for dummification in lambdastr.

3) lambdify() builds a runtime namespace mapping by collecting expr.atoms(Symbol) and adding entries namespace.update({str(term): term}) for those terms; this excludes MatrixSymbol instances from the mapping. Location: sympy/utilities/lambdify.py, top-level lambdify function (see "syms = expr.atoms(Symbol)" and subsequent namespace.update). Current expression: syms = expr.atoms(Symbol). Correct relationship: collect symbol-like objects including MatrixSymbol (for example use expr.free_symbols when available or include MatrixSymbol in the atoms call) so that symbol-like names that appear in printed code map back to SymPy objects in the exec namespace.

4) MatrixSymbol printing returns its raw name (StrPrinter._print_Symbol / _print_MatrixSymbol returns expr.name), so a MatrixSymbol created as MatrixSymbol("{v}", ...) prints as "{v}". Location: sympy/printing/str.py, StrPrinter._print_Symbol (alias _print_MatrixSymbol). Combined effect: because MatrixSymbol is not treated as symbol-like in (1) and (2), its raw name (which may be an invalid Python identifier) can be inserted verbatim into the generated def signature and cause a Python SyntaxError. The Python identifier test used elsewhere (isidentifier()/regex + keyword exclusion) is correct — the defect is that it is not applied to MatrixSymbol due to the type checks described above.
StepsToReproduce: 8) sy.lambdify(curlyv, curlyv, dummify=True)   # raises SyntaxError (dummify=True does not prevent this currently)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When dummify=True, lambdify should treat MatrixSymbol inputs whose .name contains non-identifier characters by generating and using safe dummy Python argument names (so lambdify(..., dummify=True) returns a callable rather than raising SyntaxError). When dummify=False, invalid identifier names may still cause a SyntaxError (this behavior is unchanged).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For a MatrixSymbol created as sy.MatrixSymbol("{v}", 2, 1), calling sy.lambdify(curlyv, curlyv) raises a SyntaxError because the raw name "{v}" is used verbatim in the generated def signature. Unexpectedly, sy.lambdify(curlyv, curlyv, dummify=True) also raises SyntaxError because MatrixSymbol instances are not being considered for dummification/sanitization: the lambdify code only checks for Symbol (and Function) instances and uses expr.atoms(Symbol) to populate the exec namespace, which excludes MatrixSymbol.

problem_statement.Title: LaTeX printer falls back to str() for matrices Trace (Trace(A**2) -> 'Trace(A**2)')
problem_statement.Description: Calling latex(trace(A**2)) returns the Python string 'Trace(A**2)' rather than a LaTeX rendering in which the inner expression is printed using LaTeX rules (for example, A^{2}). The LaTeX printer does not recognize the matrices Trace class and, as a result, does not invoke LaTeX-specific printing for the inner expression.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Missing LaTeX dispatch for matrices Trace: The LaTeX printer (sympy/printing/latex.py: class LatexPrinter) defines a handler for the core trace class (_print_Tr) but does not implement a handler named _print_Trace for sympy.matrices.expressions.trace.Trace. Because the printer dispatch looks for a method named _print_<ClassName> for the actual class (Trace), the absence of _print_Trace means LatexPrinter does not get control for matrix Trace objects.

2) Trace does not provide an object-level LaTeX printmethod: The matrices Trace class (sympy/matrices/expressions/trace.py: class Trace) does not implement a printmethod such as _latex. Since LatexPrinter uses printmethod = "_latex" as the first dispatch step, and Trace does not provide that method, there is no object-level print hook for Trace.

3) Printer fallback behavior leads to stringification: The generic Printer dispatch (sympy/printing/printer.py) first calls the object printmethod (if present), then searches for _print_<ClassName> methods across the expression MRO, and if no suitable printer method is found it falls back to Printer.emptyPrinter (which defaults to str). For a matrices.Trace instance this sequence results in emptyPrinter being used and the expression being converted to the Python string 'Trace(A**2)'.

4) Inner expression is not recursively printed by LatexPrinter: Because control never reaches a LaTeX-specific printer method for the matrices.Trace object, the LaTeX printer is not invoked recursively on the inner argument. The string produced by str(Trace(...)) contains the inner expression in its Python form ('A**2') instead of the LaTeX-rendered form ('A^{2}'). By contrast, existing LaTeX handlers for trace-like objects (e.g., _print_Tr) use self._print on the argument, which ensures nested expressions are formatted by the LatexPrinter.

5) Expected LaTeX relationship for Trace output: When the LaTeX printer handles a matrices.Trace instance it must print the inner expression using the printer's recursion (i.e., compute contents = self._print(expr.arg)) and then wrap it in the Trace notation. The LaTeX representation expected (diagnosis of the correct encoding) is, for example, r'\\mbox{Tr}\\left(%s\\right)' % (contents) or an equivalent operator form such as r'\\operatorname{Tr}\\left(%s\\right)'. This mirrors the existing _print_Tr implementation and ensures nested expressions like A**2 are emitted as A^{2}.
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import MatrixSymbol, trace, latex, symbols
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Return a LaTeX string representing the trace in which the inner matrix expression is rendered using LaTeX rules. Example expected output: "\\mbox{Tr}\\left(A^{2}\\right)" (or an equivalent LaTeX form such as "\\operatorname{Tr}\\left(A^{2}\\right)").
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: latex(trace(A**2)) returns the Python string 'Trace(A**2)'; the inner expression is not LaTeX-formatted (it appears as 'A**2' rather than 'A^{2}').

hints_text:
What is the correct way to print the trace? AFAIK there isn't one built in to Latex. One option is ```\mathrm{Tr}```. Or ```\operatorname{Tr}```.
What's the difference between the two. It looks like we use both in different parts of the latex printer. 
\operatorname puts a thin space after the operator.

problem_statement.Title: mathematica_code prints Max with parentheses and sorted args (invalid Mathematica syntax)
problem_statement.Description: Calling mathematica_code on a Max expression produces parentheses and a reordered argument list (e.g. 'Max(2, x)') instead of Mathematica-style square brackets with the original argument order (e.g. 'Max[x, 2]').
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. sympy/functions/elementary/miscellaneous.py: Max is implemented as a LatticeOp-derived class (Max subclasses MinMaxBase, and MinMaxBase inherits from LatticeOp). Because Max is a LatticeOp, it is dispatched to the printer method for LatticeOp objects rather than Function printers. (See MinMaxBase and Max declarations.)

2. sympy/printing/mathematica.py: class MCodePrinter does not implement a LatticeOp-specific printer method (there is no _print_LatticeOp, _print_Max, or _print_Min in MCodePrinter). MCodePrinter also sets doprint = StrPrinter.doprint, so when MCodePrinter lacks a specific _print_* override for a class, the StrPrinter/CodePrinter implementations are used. Location: MCodePrinter in sympy/printing/mathematica.py (doprint binding and _print_Function present; no LatticeOp override).

3. sympy/printing/str.py: the StrPrinter base implementation for LatticeOp sorts arguments and uses parentheses when formatting LatticeOp instances. The implementation performs args = sorted(expr.args, key=default_sort_key) and returns expr.func.__name__ + "(%s)" % ", ".join(self._print(arg) for arg in args). Because MCodePrinter does not override this for LatticeOp, Max is formatted this way, producing parentheses and a canonical (sorted) argument order.

4. sympy/printing/mathematica.py: MCodePrinter._print_Function formats Function subclasses using Mathematica-style square brackets with "%s[%s]" and preserves expr.args (it uses self.stringify(expr.args, ", ") which preserves argument order). The inconsistency is that MCodePrinter provides Mathematica formatting for Function objects but lacks a corresponding override for LatticeOp-derived classes (Max/Min), so LatticeOp objects are printed with the wrong format and ordering.

5. Effect on output: As a result of (1)-(4), calling mathematica_code(Max(x, 2)) goes through MCodePrinter.doprint (which is bound to StrPrinter.doprint) and dispatches to StrPrinter._print_LatticeOp. That produces the string "Max(2, x)" (parentheses and sorted args) while the Mathematica-valid representation expected for such a named operation is "Max[x, 2]" (square brackets and original argument order). This same root-cause pattern affects other LatticeOp-derived classes (e.g., Min) because they share the same dispatch path.
StepsToReproduce: In a Python session with sympy imported:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Return the Mathematica-valid string using square brackets and the original argument order: 'Max[x, 2]'.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Returns the string 'Max(2, x)' — uses parentheses and a sorted argument order (2 before x), which is not valid Mathematica syntax.

problem_statement.Title: trigsimp does not simplify sin(a)*sin(b) + cos(a)*cos(b) -> cos(a - b) for numeric Rational arguments (affects Matrix.dot results)
problem_statement.Description: Expressions of the form sin(a)*sin(b) + cos(a)*cos(b) are not rewritten to cos(a - b) when a and/or b are numeric Rationals (e.g. sin(1/50)*sin(1/25) + cos(1/50)*cos(1/25)). The same unsimplified form is produced when that sum arises from Matrix.dot, so calling .simplify() on the resulting scalar leaves the expression unchanged.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Incorrect conditional gate in sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py, function __trigsimp: the loop that applies the Add-pattern rewrites (matchers_add) applies TR10i(expr) and then only attempts pattern matching when expr.has(HyperbolicFunction) is True. (Location: sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py::__trigsimp, lines ~1002-1016). Because trig-only Add expressions do not have HyperbolicFunction, the trig Add patterns (e.g. c*cos(a)*cos(b) + c*sin(a)*sin(b) + d -> c*cos(a - b) + d) are never matched or applied for trig-only inputs.

2) Fragile/unsafe access of match-group 'd' in the same code path: the code evaluates res[d].atoms(...) inside the match-checking condition without first verifying that the wildcard 'd' is present in the match dictionary. If a match result contains 'a' and 'b' but not 'd', this access would raise a KeyError. (Location: sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py::__trigsimp, lines ~1011-1013).

Notes from investigation (relationships present in the code): the matching machinery intends to encode identities such as c*sin(a)*sin(b) + c*cos(a)*cos(b) + d -> c*cos(a - b) + d (matchers_add, sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py lines ~782-786). TR10i is applied before matching to normalize sum-of-products (sympy/simplify/fu.TR10i is invoked in __trigsimp at line ~1005). _dotrig(expr, pattern) is used as a preliminary compatibility filter (sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py::_dotrig, lines ~741-747). The observed incorrect behavior stems from control-flow gating (the HyperbolicFunction test) and from the fragile use of res['d'], not from absence of the identity or pattern definitions.
StepsToReproduce: python3 -c "from sympy import *; x,y = symbols('x y', real=True); r = sin(x)*sin(y) + cos(x)*cos(y); print(r); print(r.simplify())"
python3 -c "from sympy import *; t1 = Matrix([sin(Rational(1,50)), cos(Rational(1,50)), 0]); t2 = Matrix([sin(Rational(1,25)), cos(Rational(1,25)), 0]); r = t1.dot(t2); print(r); print(r.simplify())"
python3 -c "from sympy import *; r = sin(Rational(1,50))*sin(Rational(1,25)) + cos(Rational(1,50))*cos(Rational(1,25)); print(r); print(r.simplify())"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For any symbols or numeric rational arguments a and b, simplify() should rewrite sin(a)*sin(b) + cos(a)*cos(b) to cos(a - b). When the same sum is produced via Matrix.dot, the scalar result's simplify() should perform the same transformation so the printed/symbolic output is the simplified cos(a - b) form.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling .simplify() on sin(x)*sin(y) + cos(x)*cos(y) returns cos(x - y) for symbolic x,y, but for numeric Rational arguments (e.g. sin(1/50)*sin(1/25) + cos(1/50)*cos(1/25)) .simplify() leaves the expression unchanged (sin(1/50)*sin(1/25) + cos(1/50)*cos(1/25)). The same unsimplified form is produced when the sum is computed as Matrix.dot(...) and then simplified.

hints_text:
some can be simplified
```python
from sympy import *

t1 = Matrix([sin(Rational(1, 50)), cos(Rational(1, 50)), 0])
t2 = Matrix([sin(Rational(2, 50)), cos(Rational(2, 50)), 0])
t3 = Matrix([sin(Rational(3, 50)), cos(Rational(3, 50)), 0])

r1 = t1.dot(t2)
print(r1)
print(r1.simplify())
print()

r2 = t2.dot(t3)
print(r2)
print(r2.simplify())
print()
```
says
```
sin(1/50)*sin(1/25) + cos(1/50)*cos(1/25)
sin(1/50)*sin(1/25) + cos(1/50)*cos(1/25)

sin(1/25)*sin(3/50) + cos(1/25)*cos(3/50)
cos(1/50)
```
Trigonometric simplifications are performed by `trigsimp`. It works by calling sequentially functions defined in the `fu` module. This particular simplification is carried out by `TR10i` which comes right after `TRmorrie` in the [list of methods](https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/master/sympy/simplify/trigsimp.py#L1131-L1164).

`TRmorrie` does a very special type of transformation:
 
    Returns cos(x)*cos(2*x)*...*cos(2**(k-1)*x) -> sin(2**k*x)/(2**k*sin(x))

In this example, it will transform the expression into a form that `TR10i` can no more recognize.
```
>>> from sympy.simplify.fu import TRmorrie
>>> x = S(1)/50
>>> e = sin(x)*sin(2*x) + cos(x)*cos(2*x)
>>> TRmorrie(e)
sin(1/50)*sin(1/25) + sin(2/25)/(4*sin(1/50))
```
I cannot think of any reason why `TRmorrie` should come before `TR10i`. This issue could probably be fixed by changing the order of these two functions.
So, if the user-input expression varies, there is no way to simplify the expression to a very simple formation, isn't it?
I think that this issue could be fixed by changing the order of `TRmorrie` and `TR10i`. (But, of course, there may be other issues in simplification that this will not resolve.)
That should be easy to fix, assuming it works. If it doesn't work then the actual fix may be more complicated.

problem_statement.Title: LaTeX printer emits invalid leading-underscore in matrix index (double-subscript) for indexed matrix expressions
problem_statement.Description: When printing an indexed matrix expression (MatrixElement) to LaTeX, the LaTeX printer emits index names containing a raw leading underscore (for example '_i_1') inside a subscript, producing invalid LaTeX/MathJax (double-subscript) output. The symptom appears when converting expressions such as (M*N)[i, j] to LaTeX.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/latex.py -> LatexPrinter._print_MatrixElement constructs the matrix-element subscript by embedding the Python string forms of the index objects directly: '_{%s, %s}' % (expr.i, expr.j). The code path is exactly:
   def _print_MatrixElement(self, expr):
       return self.parenthesize(expr.parent, PRECEDENCE["Atom"], strict=True) \
           + '_{%s, %s}' % (expr.i, expr.j)
   This behavior inserts the raw str() of expr.i and expr.j into the LaTeX output rather than routing them through the LaTeX printer, and therefore bypasses LaTeX-specific escaping/formatting.  

2) Matrix indices (expr.i and expr.j) can be nontrivial Symbol/Dummy objects whose printed names may include leading underscores (for example Dummy('_i_1') or automatically-uniquified names that begin with '_'). MatrixElement stores indices as expr.i and expr.j (see sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py: MatrixElement defines i and j as properties). Because LatexPrinter._print_MatrixElement inserts these index objects via their raw Python string representations, leading underscores are emitted verbatim into the produced LaTeX (e.g. M_{i, _i_1} and N_{_i_1, j}), which causes LaTeX to interpret the leading underscore as an extra subscript operator and leads to invalid nested-subscript constructs.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python session with SymPy available.

Run:
  from sympy import symbols, MatrixSymbol, latex
  i, j, k = symbols('i j k')
  M = MatrixSymbol('M', k, k)
  N = MatrixSymbol('N', k, k)
  print(latex((M*N)[i, j]))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: latex((M*N)[i, j]) returns a LaTeX string that is valid LaTeX/MathJax and renders correctly; index names inside subscripts must be formatted/escaped so that no raw leading underscore is emitted that would be treated as a TeX subscript operator.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: latex((M*N)[i, j]) currently produces a string such as "\sum_{i_{1}=0}^{k - 1} M_{i, _i_1} N_{_i_1, j}". The substrings '_i_1' appear verbatim inside subscripts; LaTeX/MathJax treats the leading '_' as a subscript operator, producing an invalid nested subscript and a compilation/rendering error.

problem_statement.Title: idiff fails for Eq(...) inputs and for applied function dependent variables (f(x))
problem_statement.Description: The idiff helper in sympy/geometry/util.py rejects an applied-function dependent variable (e.g. f(x)) and does not accept Eq(...) Relational objects. As a result calling idiff with Eq(...) can raise an IndexError (solve returned no solution) and calling idiff with f(x) as the dependent variable raises a ValueError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) idiff assumes the equation argument is a plain expression (an Expr) and not a Relational (Eq). Location: sympy/geometry/util.py, idiff. Evidence: idiff never checks for or converts Relational/Eq inputs into an expression; instead it calls eq.diff(x) and passes the result to solve(...) (see lines 579 and 582). Correct relationship (diagnosis): a Relational Eq(lhs, rhs) should be treated as the expression lhs - rhs before differentiation (i.e. eq -> eq.lhs - eq.rhs) so that eq.diff(x) and subsequent symbolic solving operate on an Expr.

2) idiff assumes the dependent variable y is a Symbol (or a sequence of Symbols) and raises ValueError otherwise. Location: sympy/geometry/util.py, idiff. Evidence: the function accepts only cases where is_sequence(y) or isinstance(y, Symbol) and otherwise raises ValueError("expecting x-dependent symbol(s) but got: %s" % y) (lines 568-574). Passing an applied function such as f(x) therefore triggers this ValueError. Correct relationship (diagnosis): an x-dependent variable might be supplied as an applied function f(x); the derivative symbol to solve for in that case corresponds to Derivative(f(x), x).

3) The internal substitution/mapping logic only maps plain Symbols found in eq.free_symbols to Function(s.name)(x), so applied-function forms are not covered by the mapping. Location: sympy/geometry/util.py, idiff, where f is constructed as dict([(s, Function(s.name)(x)) for s in eq.free_symbols if s != x and s in dep]) (lines 576-577) and later reversed via yp.subs([(v, k) for k, v in f.items()]) (line 584). Evidence: because applied functions like f(x) are not Symbols and are not included in eq.free_symbols as dependent Symbols, they are not mapped to the internal Function(name)(x) form used for solving; consequently solve(eq.diff(x), dydx) targets Derivative(Function(name)(x), x) but the required mapping is not present for applied functions. Correct relationship (diagnosis): dependent identifiers should be recognized when supplied either as Symbols (y) or as applied function expressions (f(x)); if an applied function is supplied, the corresponding derivative symbol is Derivative(f(x), x) and mapping/reverse-substitution must account for the original applied form so that the solver targets the correct derivative symbol and the final result can be returned in the original input form.
StepsToReproduce: IndexError: list index out of range
raise ValueError("expecting x-dependent symbol(s) but got: %s" % y)
ValueError: expecting x-dependent symbol(s) but got: f(x)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: idiff should accept either a plain expression (Expr) or an Eq(...) Relational; if Eq is passed it should be treated as lhs - rhs for differentiation. idiff should accept a dependent variable given as a Symbol (y) or as an applied function f(x); in the latter case the solver should target Derivative(f(x), x) and the returned expression should be expressible in terms of the original input form (for example, returning results using f(x) where the user supplied f(x)). For the examples given, idiff(Eq(y*exp(y), x*exp(x)), y, x) and idiff(y*exp(y) - x*exp(x), y, x) should produce the same result; idiff(f(x)*exp(f(x)) - x*exp(x), f(x), x) should return the derivative expression expressed using f(x) (not fail).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: 1) idiff(Eq(y*exp(y), x*exp(x)), y, x) raises IndexError: list index out of range (solve returned no solution and code indexes [0]). 2) idiff(f(x)*exp(f(x)) - x*exp(x), f(x), x) raises ValueError: expecting x-dependent symbol(s) but got: f(x). 3) idiff(y*exp(y) - x*exp(x), y, x) works and returns (x + 1)*exp(x - y)/(y + 1).

problem_statement.Title: MathML printer raises TypeError when printing Indexed / IndexedBase
problem_statement.Description: Calling sympy.printing.mathml on an Indexed expression (for example IndexedBase(a)[b]) produced a TypeError in the reporter's environment: "TypeError: 'Indexed' object is not iterable". The failure also occurred inside larger expressions that contain an Indexed subexpression. The TypeError shown in the original traceback corresponds to an older mathml implementation that attempted to iterate the expression object directly; the repository version inspected iterates subexpressions via expr.args, but MathML printers still lack dedicated handlers for indexed objects, which leads to incorrect or suboptimal MathML output for Indexed/IndexedBase/Idx.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) (Historic / direct cause of the reported TypeError) The MathML printer previously assumed Basic-derived expression objects are themselves iterable and used a pattern like "for arg in e:" in _print_Basic, which raises TypeError for non-iterable classes such as Indexed/IndexedBase. This exact symptom appears in the user's traceback (TypeError: 'Indexed' object is not iterable) and indicates the code path where iteration over the expression object — rather than over its .args — was performed. In the current repository copy sympy/printing/mathml.py the Basic handlers iterate over e.args instead (see mathml.py: MathMLContentPrinter._print_Basic and MathMLPresentationPrinter._print_Basic), so that particular iteration error is fixed upstream.

2) (Current / remaining cause affecting MathML output) The MathML printers do not implement dedicated printing handlers for Indexed, IndexedBase and Idx. Location: sympy/printing/mathml.py (MathMLContentPrinter and MathMLPresentationPrinter define many type-specific _print_ methods but there are no _print_Indexed, _print_IndexedBase, or _print_Idx methods). Because Indexed objects have a defined structure (Indexed.base == args[0], Indexed.indices == args[1:]) and IndexedBase is declared NotIterable, printers that need to produce sensible MathML for indexed expressions must explicitly handle these types. The relevant data-model facts (from sympy/tensor/indexed.py) are: Indexed stores base in args[0] and indices in args[1:]; IndexedBase is declared as NotIterable (class IndexedBase(Expr, NotIterable)) and its label is available as args[0]; Idx stores its label in args[0]. The absence of explicit MathML handlers means the printers fall back to generic handling which can be incorrect or insufficient for indexed objects (other printers — e.g., latex, ccode, jscode — do implement _print_Indexed/_print_IndexedBase/_print_Idx).
StepsToReproduce: In Python REPL: import sympy; from sympy.abc import a, b
Check version: sympy.__version__  # example: '1.0.1.dev' (where the TypeError was observed)
Observe the exception: TypeError: 'Indexed' object is not iterable (in older versions where mathml iterated the expr object directly).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The MathML printer returns a MathML/XML string representing the Indexed expression (and any surrounding expression) and does not raise an exception. Indexed objects should be represented in MathML using their base and indices (i.e., a MathML representation that reflects the base identifier and its indices) and the printer should not assume expression instances themselves are iterable.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A TypeError was raised in the reporter's environment: "TypeError: 'Indexed' object is not iterable". The traceback in the original report shows the error originating from the MathML printer's _print_Basic code that attempted to iterate the expression object itself (pattern "for arg in e:"). In the repository version inspected this direct iteration has been replaced by iterating e.args; however, MathML printers still lack dedicated handlers for Indexed, IndexedBase, and Idx, so printing indexed objects currently relies on generic handlers and may produce suboptimal or semantically incorrect MathML.

problem_statement.Title: Pretty printer: Product (∏) has extra bottom blank row, overly wide box and asymmetric padding
problem_statement.Description: The pretty-printer for Product (PrettyPrinter._print_Product in sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py) builds the boxed product-sign (∏) using an ad-hoc width formula, an off-by-one vertical loop, and an asymmetric padding size. This causes (1) an extra empty line below the vertical region of ∏, (2) the ∏ to be wider than intended, and (3) visual asymmetry between top/bottom/side spacing in both Unicode and ASCII outputs.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) Off-by-one vertical segment count in PrettyPrinter._print_Product (sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py::_print_Product): the code iterates for i in range(func_height + 1) to generate the vertical bar rows under the top border; that produces one extra empty row at the bottom of the product sign. 2) Overly large hardcoded width formula in the same method: width = (func_height + 2) * 5 // 3 - 2. This formula grows relatively fast and makes the ∏ box too fat for many inputs. 3) Asymmetric padding size in the same method: padding is created with height - 1 rows (padding.stack(*[' ']*(height - 1))) instead of using the full sign height; this compounds the visual asymmetry. All three problematic constants/formulas are located in PrettyPrinter._print_Product (sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py::_print_Product). The investigation found a conservative alternative width relationship that reduces the sign thickness while guarding against non-positive widths: width = max(1, (func_height + 2) * 3 // 2 - 2). (Note: the report is diagnostic only; the code locations and the current incorrect values are listed above and the alternative formula is stated as the corrected relationship discovered during investigation.)
StepsToReproduce: In a Python REPL with SymPy imported, run:
>>> from sympy import Product, oo, symbols
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The printed Product (∏) appears visually symmetric with no extraneous blank row at the bottom; its vertical extent matches the mathematical content being attached and the horizontal width of the product box is not excessively wide (i.e., slimmer and visually more consistent with the Sum/∑ printer). Unicode and ASCII renderings should both be symmetric and not contain an extra blank line under the vertical area.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
The printed Product contains an empty line at the bottom of the ∏ vertical region, the ∏ box is wider than desirable, and the overall shape looks asymmetric (top bar and vertical sides produce a larger visual footprint than necessary). Example (unicode):
  ∞
┬───┬
│   │ 1
│   │
 n = 1
(there is an extra blank line before 'n = 1' and the ∏ box is relatively fat)

problem_statement.Title: Sum pretty-print baseline overwritten causing misaligned '+' when Sum is added to other terms
problem_statement.Description: When a Sum is pretty-printed as part of an Add (e.g. Sum(...) + 3), the operator and following term ("+ 3") are vertically misaligned by one line relative to the summand inside the sum sign. The misalignment is produced by the Sum pretty-printer's baseline value being changed during assembly and then later unconditionally overwritten when the Sum prettyForm is completed.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py — PrettyPrinter._print_Sum: The method uses an asum helper to compute the visual shape/height of the sum sign and, during assembly, adjusts the inner function's baseline to center the summand against the sign. The centering step is implemented here (intended to place the summand on the visual row inside the sign):
   # change F baseline so it centers on the sign
   prettyF.baseline -= d - (prettyF.height()//2 - prettyF.baseline) - adjustment
   (PrettyPrinter._print_Sum, lines 619–623).

2) sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py — PrettyPrinter._print_Sum: After building the sign and placing the (already adjusted) summand to the right, the method then unconditionally overwrites the assembled baseline with the expression
   prettyF.baseline = max_upper + sign_height//2
   (PrettyPrinter._print_Sum, line 632).
   This assignment discards the previously computed baseline that had been adjusted to center the summand relative to the graphic of the sum sign; the overwritten value is the explicit expression that the pretty-form returns as its baseline.

3) sympy/printing/pretty/stringpict.py — stringPict.next / prettyForm.__add__: Composition logic determines the alignment baseline for a concatenation by taking the maximum baseline of the components (newBaseline = max(obj.baseline for obj in objects)) and padding each component so its baseline equals newBaseline (stringPict.next, lines 69–86). prettyForm.__add__ calls stringPict.next when creating the Add prettyForm (stringpict.prettyForm.__add__, lines 393–405). Because Add alignment uses the Sum prettyForm.baseline as the reference, any incorrect final baseline returned by _print_Sum directly shifts the position of the '+' and subsequent term vertically. In the reported case the final baseline value (see item 2) does not match the visual row where the summand was placed by the earlier centering step, so '+' and '3' are aligned to the wrong row.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL with SymPy available.
from sympy import symbols, Sum, pprint, oo
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The '+' operator and the following term ('+ 3') are vertically aligned with the summand inside the sum sign (the '+' should appear on the same horizontal row as the 'x' inside the sum graphic).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
The '+' and '3' are drawn one line lower than the summand inside the sum sign. Example output shown in the report:
  ∞
 ___
 ╲
  ╲   x
  ╱     + 3
 ╱
 ‾‾‾
x = 1

problem_statement.Title: autowrap (cython backend) emits scalar parameter for unused MatrixSymbol arguments, causing TypeError
problem_statement.Description: When autowrap is used with the Cython backend and the user supplies an array/matrix argument that does not appear in the wrapped expression, the generated C signature treats that argument as a scalar rather than an array/pointer. The mismatch causes the Python->C wrapper to pass a numpy.ndarray where the C function expects a scalar, leading to a runtime TypeError.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) CodeGen.routine (sympy/utilities/codegen.py: CodeGen.routine) — when argument_sequence is supplied, the routine reconstructs the requested argument order by looking up symbols discovered earlier. If a requested symbol is not present in the arguments previously discovered from the expressions, the fallback creates InputArgument(symbol) with no dimensions metadata. Concretely, the base routine builds array_symbols only from symbols that actually occur in the expressions (e.g. expressions.atoms(MatrixSymbol)) (see sympy/utilities/codegen.py lines ~698-703 and ~704-714), but when a user-supplied symbol is missing from the discovered arguments it is created via InputArgument(symbol) in the KeyError except block (sympy/utilities/codegen.py:736-743). This encodes the implicit assumption "missing-from-expression => no dimensions (scalar)".

2) Language-specific routine implementations (sympy/utilities/codegen.py: JuliaCodeGen.routine, OctaveCodeGen.routine, RustCodeGen.routine) — the same KeyError fallback is repeated in these overrides: when a user-requested symbol is not found in the local name->argument mapping they call InputArgument(symbol) without dimensions (see sympy/utilities/codegen.py lines ~1401-1406 for Julia, ~1609-1614 for Octave, ~1841-1845 for Rust). Each of these locations therefore encodes the same incorrect fallback assumption as (1).

3) Downstream components rely solely on Argument.dimensions to decide array vs scalar (sympy/utilities/codegen.py: CCodeGen.get_prototype and sympy/utilities/autowrap.py: CythonCodeWrapper.dump_pyx / _prototype_arg / _call_arg) — these components emit pointer/ndarray signatures only when arg.dimensions is present. For example, CCodeGen.get_prototype uses "if arg.dimensions or isinstance(arg, ResultBase): emit pointer signature" (sympy/utilities/codegen.py:911-916). The Cython wrapper uses arg.dimensions to declare numpy.ndarray arguments in the .pyx (sympy/utilities/autowrap.py: _prototype_arg, lines ~439-451) and to pass a data pointer to the C function (sympy/utilities/autowrap.py: _call_arg, lines ~461-464). Because the fallbacks in (1)/(2) produce InputArgument instances with no dimensions metadata, the generated C prototype and the pyx wrapper use scalar signatures (e.g., "double autofunc(double x)") instead of pointer/ndarray signatures.

4) Exact representation used by the C/Cython path: when an argument is treated as an array (MatrixSymbol or Indexed), the dimensions metadata is constructed in the base routine as a sequence of tuples [(S.Zero, dim - 1) for dim in symbol.shape] (see sympy/utilities/codegen.py: lines ~663, ~669, ~709). Presence of such dimensions metadata is what causes prototype/wrapper code generation to emit pointer/ndarray signatures; absence implies a scalar signature. Note: some language-specific generators use a different encoding appropriate to the target language (for example, JuliaCodeGen uses (S.One, dim) in some places because of 1-based indexing), so the exact tuple encoding is language-specific; for the C/Cython backend the correct encoding is (S.Zero, dim - 1).
StepsToReproduce: Create a MatrixSymbol and a constant expression, e.g. x = MatrixSymbol('x', 2, 1); expr = 1.0
Call autowrap with the cython backend and include x in args: f = autowrap(expr, args=(x,), backend='cython')
Invoke the wrapped function with a numpy array for x, e.g. f(np.array([[1.0, 2.0]]))
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The wrapped function accepts a numpy.ndarray for the MatrixSymbol argument and returns the correct value (for the example, return 1.0). The generated C prototype should use an array/pointer parameter for matrix arguments and the pyx wrapper should declare a numpy.ndarray argument.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The call raises: TypeError: only size-1 arrays can be converted to Python scalars. The generated C function signature for the example is scalar (e.g. "double autofunc(double x)") instead of a pointer/array (e.g. "double autofunc(double *x)").

problem_statement.Title: Intersection simplification yields different result when duplicate set arguments are present
problem_statement.Description: Providing duplicate arguments to Intersection changes the simplification outcome. Example: Intersection({1}, {1}, {x}) returns EmptySet() while Intersection({1}, {x}) returns {1}; duplicates should not change the mathematical result.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Intersection.__new__ (sympy/sets/sets.py::Intersection.__new__) in its evaluate=True branch constructs the argument list as list(cls._new_args_filter(args)) and immediately calls simplify_intersection(args). This preserves duplicate arguments for the simplifier. (See Intersection.__new__ evaluate=True: "args = list(cls._new_args_filter(args)); return simplify_intersection(args)".)

2) simplify_intersection (sympy/sets/sets.py::simplify_intersection) invokes Intersection._handle_finite_sets(args) before any global deduplication (e.g. before converting args to a set). Because the finite-set handler receives the original list, duplicates in the argument list are visible to the multi-FiniteSet logic. (See simplify_intersection: "rv = Intersection._handle_finite_sets(args); if rv is not None: return rv".)

3) Intersection._handle_finite_sets (sympy/sets/sets.py::Intersection._handle_finite_sets) gathers all FiniteSet arguments into fs_args, sorts by length, selects the smallest fs_args[0] as the reference, and uses the remaining fs_args to refine results. The routine mutates and iterates over fs_args (e.g. fs_args = fs_args[1:]; fs_args.pop()), and appends modified sets to other; it does not deduplicate fs_args. As a result, duplicate FiniteSet arguments in the input list can change the reference/ordering/heuristics used by this code path and lead to different (incorrect) simplification outcomes for semantically idempotent inputs.

4) By contrast, the generic lattice-operation constructor LatticeOp.__new__ (sympy/core/operations.py::LatticeOp.__new__) canonicalizes arguments using frozenset(cls._new_args_filter(args)), thereby: (a) flattening nested same-class ops, (b) deduplicating arguments (idempotence), and (c) short-circuiting on presence of the absorbing element (ShortCircuit -> cls.zero). LatticeOp.__new__ uses frozenset(cls._new_args_filter(args)) and handles ShortCircuit; see the lines where it constructs _args = frozenset(cls._new_args_filter(args)) and returns cls.zero if ShortCircuit is raised. Intersection.__new__ evaluate=True path does not reproduce these canonicalization/short-circuit semantics before calling simplify_intersection, creating the inconsistency.

5) Note: Intersection.__new__ evaluate=False already orders args and stores obj._argset = frozenset(args) (sympy/sets/sets.py::Intersection.__new__ evaluate=False branch). Thus deduplication is performed when the object is being created in the evaluate=False path; the missing deduplication is specific to the evaluate=True simplification path that calls simplify_intersection.

Summary: simplify_intersection runs finite-set special-case logic before any canonicalization/deduplication. Intersection.__new__ evaluate=True passes a possibly-duplicated list to simplify_intersection, so duplicated FiniteSet arguments can affect the finite-set heuristics in _handle_finite_sets and produce differing outcomes for inputs that are mathematically idempotent. The canonical argument collection used by lattice ops is represented by frozenset(cls._new_args_filter(args)); the Intersection evaluate=True path fails to apply that canonicalization before simplification.
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import Intersection, FiniteSet, symbols
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Duplicate arguments to Intersection are ignored (idempotence). Intersection({1}, {1}, {x}) should return the same user-visible result as Intersection({1}, {x}) — for example, either Piecewise(({1}, Eq(x, 1)), (S.EmptySet, True)) or an unevaluated Intersection that is equivalent to Intersection({1}, {x}).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Intersection(FiniteSet(1), FiniteSet(1), FiniteSet(x)) evaluates to EmptySet() while Intersection(FiniteSet(1), FiniteSet(x)) evaluates to {1}; the extra duplicate {1} changes the result.

problem_statement.Title: lambdify emits 'I' for Matrix Identity causing it to be interpreted as imaginary unit (1j) for NumPy lambdify
problem_statement.Description: When lambdify is used with NumPy, Matrix Identity objects (Identity(n)) are printed as the token 'I' in the generated Python source. lambdify's NumPy runtime namespace binds 'I' to the complex imaginary unit (1j), so the generated function uses 1j instead of an identity matrix, producing incorrect numeric results.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/printing/str.py : StrPrinter._print_Identity returns the single-letter string "I" (see sympy/printing/str.py: _print_Identity -> return "I"). This causes a Matrix Identity to be emitted as the bare token 'I' by any printer that inherits this method.

2) sympy/utilities/lambdify.py : NUMPY_DEFAULT = {"I": 1j} (and SCIPY_DEFAULT = {"I": 1j}). lambdify constructs the execution globals for the numeric modules using these mappings, so an unqualified token 'I' in the generated function will be resolved at runtime to Python's complex imaginary unit 1j.

3) sympy/printing/pycode.py : NumPyPrinter (used by lambdify for the 'numpy' module) does not implement a dedicated _print_Identity, so it inherits StrPrinter._print_Identity (item 1). As a result, when lambdify uses the NumPyPrinter to generate code for expressions containing Identity, the emitted code contains the bare token 'I' which, per item 2, is bound to 1j at runtime.

4) The same root-cause pattern applies to other code printers that do not override _print_Identity: any printer used for code generation that inherits StrPrinter._print_Identity will output 'I' for Identity and therefore risks misinterpretation when lambdify populates the function globals with numeric module mappings that bind 'I' to the imaginary unit (for example, SciPy's default namespace also maps 'I' to 1j and SciPyPrinter subclasses NumPyPrinter). In short, the problem is the interaction between (a) a printer emitting the ambiguous literal 'I' for a matrix identity and (b) lambdify's numeric namespaces binding the same name to the imaginary unit.

5) Correct semantic for numeric code generation (diagnostic statement): in numeric-targeted generated code (e.g., when the module is 'numpy'), an Identity of size rows must be represented unambiguously as an expression that evaluates to an identity matrix at runtime (for example, a call equivalent to numpy.eye(rows) or an import-registered eye(rows) call), not the bare symbol 'I'. This statement documents what the generated code must represent, not how to implement it.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sympy import symbols, MatrixSymbol, Identity, lambdify; import numpy as np; n = symbols('n', integer=True); A = MatrixSymbol('A', n, n); a = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]]); f = lambdify(A, A + Identity(n)); print(f(a))"
Alternatively, in an interactive Python session:
>>> from sympy import symbols, MatrixSymbol, Identity, lambdify
>>> f(a)  # shows complex entries due to 'I' -> 1j mapping
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The lambdified function should perform numeric matrix addition with an identity matrix: adding Identity(n) to a numpy array should add 1.0 on the diagonal entries and leave off-diagonal entries unchanged. For the example input, f(a) should return array([[2,2],[3,5]]).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated function treats the printed token 'I' as the complex imaginary unit (1j). For the example, f(a) returns array([[1.+1.j, 2.+1.j],[3.+1.j, 4.+1.j]]), i.e. each element has 1j added instead of adding 1 on the diagonal.

hints_text: If the shape is an explicit number, we can just print `eye(n)`. For unknown shape, it's harder. We can raise an exception for now. It's better to raise an exception than give a wrong answer.

problem_statement.Title: simplify(cos(x)**I) raises TypeError: Invalid comparison of complex I in fu._TR56
problem_statement.Description: Calling simplify(cos(x)**I) triggers a TypeError originating in the integer-specific power transformation code in sympy/simplify/fu.py when the exponent is the imaginary unit I.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/simplify/fu.py::_TR56 (inner function _f): _f performs numeric comparisons on the exponent using expressions like (rv.exp < 0) and (rv.exp > max) without first ensuring rv.exp is comparable to real numbers. The code also uses integer-specific checks and arithmetic on the exponent (rv.exp == 2, rv.exp == 4, rv.exp % 2, rv.exp//2, and perfect_power(rv.exp)). These lines encode the incorrect assumption that rv.exp is always a real/integer value.  

2) The integer-specific operations listed above are only valid when the exponent is known to be an integer (i.e., rv.exp.is_integer is True). When rv.exp is a complex value (for example S.ImaginaryUnit), these operations are not appropriate and attempting numeric comparisons invokes Expr comparison machinery that rejects complex vs real comparisons.  

3) sympy/core/expr.py::Expr.__lt__ (and related comparison methods): comparing a complex expression (e.g. S.ImaginaryUnit) to a real value triggers a TypeError("Invalid comparison of complex %s" % me). Because _TR56._f does unguarded comparisons like (rv.exp < 0) when rv.exp is I, Expr.__lt__ raises the TypeError observed in the traceback.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sympy import *; x = Symbol('x'); simplify(cos(x)**I)"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling simplify(cos(x)**I) should not raise an exception. The operation should return a SymPy expression (for example, leave cos(x)**I unchanged or return a valid simplification) rather than raising TypeError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A TypeError is raised: "Invalid comparison of complex I". The exception originates when fu._TR56._f in sympy/simplify/fu.py attempts to evaluate (rv.exp < 0) (and/or other numeric checks or integer-only operations) while rv.exp is S.ImaginaryUnit, causing Expr.__lt__ to raise.

problem_statement.Title: AttributeError when multiplying BlockMatrix containing ZeroMatrix blocks (scalar Zero appears as block)
problem_statement.Description: When multiplying a BlockMatrix that contains ZeroMatrix blocks more than once (e.g. b * b * b), an AttributeError is raised because one of the block entries becomes the scalar sympy.core.numbers.Zero instead of a ZeroMatrix; later BlockMatrix code expects blocks to be matrix objects with .rows/.cols.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) BlockMatrix._blockmul (sympy/matrices/expressions/blockmatrix.py) constructs a result block matrix by directly using the product of the underlying explicit matrix of blocks: it returns BlockMatrix(self.blocks * other.blocks) when block sizes align. This delegates per-block computation to the dense matrix multiplication code and therefore to scalar Add/Mul construction.

2) BlockMatrix._blockadd (sympy/matrices/expressions/blockmatrix.py) does the analogous thing for addition: it returns BlockMatrix(self.blocks + other.blocks) when the two BlockMatrix operands are structurally compatible. This uses the underlying DenseMatrix elementwise addition and exposes the same risk of scalar-valued entries appearing in place of matrix objects.

3) DenseMatrix elementwise construction for multiplication/addition (sympy/matrices/dense.py) computes each resulting element via Add(*vec) (with a reduce fallback): this is the exact construction point where a block-entry expression is built as a SymPy expression. Because Add/Mul simplification operates at the scalar-expression level, an entry that is semantically a zero-matrix can simplify to the scalar sympy.core.numbers.Zero along some simplification paths. The relevant code is the per-element Add(*vec) used in DenseMatrix._eval_matrix_mul.

4) MatMul canonicalization contains a rule that intends zero-valued matrix products to be represented as a ZeroMatrix (sympy/matrices/expressions/matmul.py: any_zeros returns ZeroMatrix(matrices[0].rows, matrices[-1].cols) if any factor is zero). That rule shows the intended semantic: a zero block should be a ZeroMatrix with explicit shape. However, the explicit DenseMatrix construction described in (3) can nonetheless yield a scalar Zero in the block slot rather than a ZeroMatrix.

5) Several BlockMatrix properties and methods assume every block entry is a matrix object (MatrixExpr / MatrixBase) exposing .rows and .cols. In particular, colblocksizes/rowblocksizes are implemented as [self.blocks[0, i].cols ...] / [self.blocks[i, 0].rows ...]; if an entry is the scalar Zero, accessing .cols/.rows raises AttributeError. BlockMatrix.__new__ also enforces that constructor arguments be matrices (is_Matrix True), so scalar block entries violate BlockMatrix's invariants.

6) Correct relationship / invariant that is being violated: for block indices (i, j) the block entry produced by blockwise multiplication or addition must be a matrix object. If the computed entry represents the zero matrix, it must be the ZeroMatrix of the appropriate shape, i.e. ZeroMatrix(self.rowblocksizes[i], other.colblocksizes[j]) (or the corresponding row/col sizes for addition), not the scalar Zero. The observed symptom (e.g. type(b._blockmul(b).blocks[0, 1]) is sympy.core.numbers.Zero) shows this invariant was violated.
StepsToReproduce: block_collapse(b * b * b)   # raises AttributeError: 'Zero' object has no attribute 'cols'
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Repeated block-multiplication and collapse of a BlockMatrix that contains ZeroMatrix blocks should succeed and produce a BlockMatrix / explicit Matrix whose zero blocks are represented as ZeroMatrix objects (with the correct block shape), so subsequent block-aware operations can read .rows/.cols. For the example, block_collapse(b * b * b) should not raise an exception and should yield block entries that are ZeroMatrix where appropriate.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: After one block multiply the off-diagonal block may become the scalar Zero (sympy.core.numbers.Zero). On a second multiplication code that reads block sizes (colblocksizes / rowblocksizes) does self.blocks[0, i].cols and crashes with AttributeError: 'Zero' object has no attribute 'cols'. The exception trace originates from block_collapse -> bc_matmul -> BlockMatrix._blockmul -> colblocksizes, as reported.

problem_statement.Title: Reverse multiplication with geometry.Point delegates to left operand and returns Mul (e.g. 2.0*Point) instead of Point
problem_statement.Description: When evaluating expressions of the form number * Point, Python dispatch ends up returning a Mul (e.g. 2.0*Point2D(1,1)) rather than invoking the Point multiplication logic so a geometry object is produced. That Mul is not a Point and later geometry operations (e.g. Point.__add__) raise exceptions when they attempt to interpret the Mul as point coordinates.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) GeometryEntity implements reverse operators by delegating to the left operand's operator. For example, in sympy/geometry/entity.py the reverse operator implementations use the left operand's method:
   - __radd__: return a.__add__(self)
   - __rdiv__: return a.__div__(self)
   - __rmul__: return a.__mul__(self)
   - __rsub__: return a.__sub__(self)
   This encodes the assumption that the left operand will perform the operation correctly when the right operand is a geometry entity.

2) Numeric left operands (e.g. sympy Float) do not implement multiplication with geometry.Point so as to return a geometry object. Float.__mul__ delegates to atomic multiplication logic which constructs a Mul expression (2.0*Point2D(1,1)) rather than invoking Point.__mul__ to produce a Point. (Location: sympy/core/numbers.py, Float.__mul__.)

3) Point addition/methods expect operands that can be converted to a Point. Point.__add__ calls Point(other, evaluate=False) and, if other is not a sequence or a Point, the Point constructor raises TypeError ("Expecting sequence of coordinates, not `...`"), which Point.__add__ converts to a GeometryError. Therefore, when reverse multiplication yields a Mul AST instead of a Point, subsequent geometry operations fail. (Locations: sympy/geometry/point.py, Point.__add__ and Point.__new__.)

4) The concrete manifestation of the incorrect assumption is that __rmul__ (and the other reverse operators implemented the same way) defer to the left operand even when the right-hand geometry object already defines an appropriate __mul__ (or other operator) that would produce a geometry object. This dispatch choice is what causes number * Point to produce a Mul instead of the Point that Point.__mul__ would produce.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sympy import geometry as ge, sympify; p1 = ge.Point(0,0); p2 = ge.Point(1,1); print(p1 + p2*sympify(2.0))"  # works
python -c "from sympy import geometry as ge, sympify; p1 = ge.Point(0,0); p2 = ge.Point(1,1); print(p1 + sympify(2.0)*p2)"  # raises GeometryError / TypeError
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: number * Point and Point * number yield equivalent results (both produce a Point with coordinates multiplied by the number). Expressions combining such products with other geometry operations (e.g. point + (number * point2)) should not raise exceptions and should produce the same result regardless of operand order.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: number * Point returns a Mul expression (e.g. 2.0*Point2D(1,1)) instead of a Point. Subsequent geometry operations that expect a Point raise TypeError / GeometryError because the Mul cannot be interpreted as a sequence of coordinates.

hints_text: You can multiply a Point on the right by a scalar but not on the left. I think this would be a matter of defining `__rmul__` for Point.

problem_statement.Title: sympify parses repr() of non-string objects; Expr.__eq__ triggers eval of repr leading to AttributeError and incorrect equality
problem_statement.Description: When comparing a SymPy Expr to an arbitrary Python object, SymPy's sympify() will take the object's repr/unicode and parse/eval it as Python code. Because parse_expr ultimately runs eval(code, global_dict, local_dict), this causes eval() to execute code derived from an object's repr during equality checks (Expr.__eq__), which can raise exceptions (e.g. AttributeError) or produce spurious equality results.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/sympify.py :: sympify(a, ...)
   - Implementation fact (lines 365-369, 383-386): when earlier conversion attempts fail, sympify unconditionally does:
       a = unicode(a)
       expr = parse_expr(a, ...)
   - Incorrect / unsafe assumption: sympify treats an arbitrary object's unicode()/repr() as a legitimate expression to parse and evaluate. This allows arbitrary object reprs (e.g. 'x' or 'x.y') to be interpreted as SymPy expressions.

2) sympy/parsing/sympy_parser.py :: eval_expr(code, local_dict, global_dict)
   - Implementation fact (lines 896-904): eval_expr performs:
       expr = eval(code, global_dict, local_dict)
   - Consequence: strings produced from an object's repr that reach parse_expr are executed via eval in SymPy's environment, enabling attribute access (e.g. Symbol('x').y) or other evaluation side effects.

3) sympy/core/expr.py :: Expr.__eq__(self, other)
   - Implementation fact (lines 122-128): Expr.__eq__ calls sympify(other) (non-strict) and will catch SympifyError/SyntaxError and return False.
   - Consequence: equality checks between a SymPy Expr and an arbitrary object will trigger sympify's last-resort parsing of that object's repr, leading to the unsafe parse/eval behavior described above.

4) sympy/core/sympify.py :: _sympify and decorator usage (context)
   - Implementation facts: _sympify() returns sympify(a, strict=True) (line 418); the @_sympifyit decorator calls sympify(..., strict=True) (decorators.py). That means callers using _sympify/strict sympify already avoid the repr-parsing fallback. The mismatch in behavior between strict and non-strict sympify explains why Basic.__eq__ (which uses _sympify) would not have triggered this, while Expr.__eq__ (which calls sympify non-strict) does.

5) Correct relationship (diagnostic statement about the incorrect behavior)
   - The unsafe behavior is the unconditional conversion to unicode() and subsequent parsing for arbitrary non-string objects. The diagnostic (concrete) relationship that describes the correct/safer behavior is:
       Only call parse_expr(unicode(a)) (and thus eval code derived from a) when the original input 'a' is already a string instance (i.e., isinstance(a, string_types)). If the input is not a string and all other conversion routes (converters, _sympy_, numeric coercion, iterable handling, etc.) are exhausted, sympify should raise SympifyError rather than attempt to parse the object's repr/unicode.
   - Expressed concretely in code logic (diagnosis of what is wrong and the correct conditional):
       # Current (unsafe):
       a = unicode(a)
       expr = parse_expr(a, ...)
       # Correct/safer relation (diagnosis of the intended check):
       if not isinstance(a, string_types):
           raise SympifyError(a)
       s = unicode(a)
       expr = parse_expr(s, ...)
   - Note: this is a statement of the correct relationship/guard that must hold to avoid parsing arbitrary object reprs; it is a diagnosis of the incorrect behavior in the current code rather than a prescriptive patch description.
StepsToReproduce: 1) Run Python and import sympy: import sympy
2) Create an object whose __repr__ returns an attribute access string:
- This raises: AttributeError: 'Symbol' object has no attribute 'y' (trace shows eval of code produced from 'x.y').
4) (Related) Create an object whose __repr__ returns a bare symbol string:
assert sympy.Symbol('x') != D()  # currently fails because D() may be parsed as Symbol('x')
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Comparing a SymPy expression to an arbitrary non-SymPy Python object should not evaluate the object's repr. Equality should return False for unrelated objects and must not trigger eval() of the object's repr or attribute accesses. sympify() should only parse strings that were explicitly provided as strings by the caller.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: sympify() takes the repr/unicode of arbitrary objects and passes it to the parser. parse_expr ultimately evaluates the generated code (via eval), causing attribute accesses and exceptions (e.g. AttributeError from Symbol('x').y) and allowing objects with repr 'x' to be interpreted as the same Symbol('x') and therefore compare equal.

hints_text:
See also #12524
Safe flag or no, == should call _sympify since an expression shouldn't equal a string. 

I also think we should deprecate the string fallback in sympify. It has led to serious performance issues in the past and clearly has security issues as well. 
Actually, it looks like we also have

```
>>> x == 'x'
True
```

which is a major regression since 1.4. 

I bisected it to 73caef3991ca5c4c6a0a2c16cc8853cf212db531. 

The bug in the issue doesn't exist in 1.4 either. So we could consider doing a 1.5.1 release fixing this. 
The thing is, I could have swore this behavior was tested. But I don't see anything in the test changes from https://github.com/sympy/sympy/pull/16924 about string comparisons.
