Thank you both so much!
As a general rule of thumb, pretty much any function that takes a SymPy expression as input and manipulates it in some way (simplify, solve, integrate, etc.) is liable to give wrong answers if the expression was created with evaluate=False. There is code all over the place that assumes, either explicitly or implicitly, that expressions satisfy various conditions that are true after automatic evaluation happens. For example, if I am reading the PR correctly, there is some code that very reasonably assumes that Mul.make_args(expr) gives the terms of a multiplication. This is not true for evaluate=False because that disables flattening of arguments. 

If you are working with expressions created with evaluate=False, you should always evaluate them first before trying to pass them to functions like simplify(). The result of simplify would be evaluated anyway, so there's no reason to not do this.

This isn't to say I'm necessarily opposed to fixing this issue specifically, but in general I think fixes like this are untenable. There are a handful of things that should definitely work correctly with unevaluated expressions, like the printers, and some very basic expression manipulation functions. I'm less convinced it's a good idea to try to enforce this for something like the assumptions or high level simplification functions. 

This shows we really need to rethink how we represent unevaluated expressions in SymPy. The fact that you can create an expression that looks just fine, but is actually subtly "invalid" for some code is indicative that something is broken in the design. It would be better if unevaluated expressions were more explicitly separate from evaluated ones. Or if expressions just didn't evaluate as much. I'm not sure what the best solution is, just that the current situation isn't ideal.
I think that the real issue is the fact that `fraction` is not a suitable function to use within the core assumptions system. I'm sure I objected to it being introduced somewhere.

The core assumptions should be able to avoid giving True or False erroneously because of unevaluated expressions.
In fact here's a worse form of the bug:
```julia
In [1]: x = Symbol('x', rational=True)                                                                                                                                            

In [2]: fraction(x)                                                                                                                                                               
Out[2]: (x, 1)

In [3]: y = Symbol('y', rational=True)                                                                                                                                            

In [4]: (x*y).is_integer                                                                                                                                                          
Out[4]: True
```

This looks like the right place to not only decide if an expression evaluates to an integer, but also check if the decision is feasible (i.e. possible without full evaluation).

Yes, I think such tests/intentions should be dropped: if an expression was constructed with `evaluate=False`, the integer decision can be given up, if it cannot be decided without evaluation.

problem_statement.Title: convert_to yields fractional / nonsensical unit exponents when target units don't exactly match (e.g. joule*second -> joule gives joule**(7/9))
problem_statement.Description: Calling convert_to on a unit expression that contains orthogonal / extra dimensions (for example joule*second) with a target unit set that cannot exactly represent those dimensions (for example just joule) can produce a fractional-power unit result (e.g., joule**(7/9)) instead of refusing the conversion or returning the original expression.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/physics/units/util.py:_get_conversion_matrix_for_expr constructs a linear system that maps target unit canonical-dimension columns (camat) to the expression's canonical-dimension vector (exprmat) but solves that system using Matrix.solve_least_squares and returns the resulting exponent vector unconditionally. The location of the least-squares call is: util._get_conversion_matrix_for_expr (calls camat.solve_least_squares and returns res_exponents).

2) sympy/physics/units/util.py:convert_to (and thereby Quantity.convert_to in sympy/physics/units/quantities.py which delegates to util.convert_to) accepts the exponent vector returned by _get_conversion_matrix_for_expr without verifying that it is an exact solution of the dimensional system. convert_to then constructs the returned expression by raising target units to those exponents, so an inexact least-squares solution becomes fractional/rational powers of unit symbols.

3) The implicit correctness criterion used by the conversion logic is that the exponent vector must satisfy the linear system exactly: camat * res_exponents == exprmat. When that equality does not hold, no exponent vector exists that exactly decomposes the expression into the requested target units; treating a least-squares approximate solution as valid leads to nonsensical unit expressions. (This incorrect acceptance of approximate solutions is encoded by the use of solve_least_squares and the unconditional return of its result in _get_conversion_matrix_for_expr.)

4) All occurrences of this root-cause pattern in the units conversion flow are in the pair of functions noted above: util._get_conversion_matrix_for_expr (computes and returns least-squares exponents) and util.convert_to (consumes and uses them). Quantity.convert_to simply delegates to util.convert_to and thus is affected equally.
StepsToReproduce: In a SymPy environment (tested on sympy 1.4):
>>> from sympy.physics.units import convert_to, joule, second
Other examples that behave sensibly (for reference):
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When the provided target units cannot exactly represent the dimensions of the expression, convert_to should not return a fractional or otherwise invalid power of the target unit. The user-visible outcome should be either the original expression unchanged or a clear conversion-failure result. For conversions that are exact, convert_to should return the equivalent expression in the requested target units.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: convert_to accepts a least-squares exponent solution for an infeasible dimensional system and returns fractional-unit powers (for example, convert_to(joule*second, joule) returns joule**(7/9)), producing nonsensical unit expressions when the target units do not exactly match the expression's dimensions.

hints_text:
Yes, this is a problem. When trying to convert into a unit that is not compatible, it should either do nothing (no conversion), or raise an exception. I personally don't see how the following makes sense:
```
>>> convert_to(meter, second) 
meter

```
I often do calculations with units as a failsafe check. When The operation checks out and delivers reasonable units, I take it as a sign that it went well. When it "silently" converts an expression into non-sensible units, this cannot be used as a failsafe check.
I am glad someone agrees this is a problem. I suggest that the physics.units package be disabled for now as it has serious flaws.

My solution is simply to use positive, real symbolic variables for units. I worry about the conversions myself. For example: `var('J m kg s Pa', positive=True, real=True)`. These behave as proper units and don't do anything mysterious. For unit conversions, I usually just use things like `.subs({J:kg*m**2/s**2})`. You could also use substitution using `.evalf()`.
> I suggest that the physics.units package be disabled for now

That seems a little drastic.

I don't use the units module but the docstring for `convert_to` says:
```
    Convert ``expr`` to the same expression with all of its units and quantities
    represented as factors of ``target_units``, whenever the dimension is compatible.
```
There are examples in the docstring showing that the `target_units` parameter can be a list and is intended to apply only to the relevant dimensions e.g.:
```
In [11]: convert_to(3*meter/second, hour)                                                                                                      
Out[11]: 
10800⋅meter
───────────
    hour
```
If you want a function to convert between strictly between one compound unit and another or otherwise raise an error then that seems reasonable but it probably needs to be a different function (maybe there already is one).
Hi @oscarbenjamin ! Thanks for your leads and additional information provided. I am relatively new to this and have to have a deeper look at the docstring. (Actually, I had a hard time finding the right information. I was mainly using google and did not get far enough.)
I stand by my suggestion. As my first example shows in the initial entry 
for this issue the result from a request that should return the original 
expression unchanged provides a wrong answer. This is exactly equivalent 
to the example you give, except that the particular case is wrong. As 
@schniepp shows there are other cases. This module needs repair and is 
not safely usable unless you know the answer you should get.

I think the convert_to function needs fixing. I would call this a bug. I 
presently do not have time to figure out how to fix it. If somebody does 
that would be great, but if not I think leaving it active makes SymPy's 
quality control look poor.

If the module is usable for anything then people will be using it so we can't just disable it.

In any case I'm sure it would be easier to fix the problem than it would be to disable the module.
Can we then please mark this as a bug, so it will receive some priority.
I've marked it as a bug but that doesn't imply any particular priority. Priority just comes down to what any contributor wants to work on.

I suspect that there are really multiple separate issues here but someone needs to take the time to investigate the causes to find out.
I agree that this is probably an indicator of multiple issues. My quick look at the code suggested there is something odd about the way the basis is handled and that I was not going to find a quick fix. Thus I went back to just treating units as symbols as I do in hand calculations. For teaching, I've concluded that is better anyway.
I also ran into this issue and wanted  to share my experience.  I ran this command and got the following result. 

```
>>> convert_to(5*ohm*2*A**2/cm, watt*m)
1000*10**(18/19)*meter**(13/19)*watt**(13/19)
```

The result is obviously meaningless.  I spent a lot of time trying to figure out what was going on.  I finally figured out the mistake was on my end.  I typed 'watt*m' for the target unit when what I wanted was 'watt/m.'  This is a problem mostly because if the user does not catch their mistake right away they are going to assume the program is not working.
> I suggest that the physics.units package be disabled for now as it has serious flaws.

If we disable the module in the master branch, it will only become available after a new SymPy version release. At that point, we will be bombarded by millions of people complaining about the missing module on Github and Stackoverflow.

Apparently, `physics.units` is one of the most used modules in SymPy. We keep getting lots of complaints even for small changes.
@Upabjojr I understand your reasoning. It still does not address the root problem of something wrong in how the basis set of units is handled. Could somebody at least update the instructions for `convert_to` to clearly warn about how it fails. 

I have other projects, so do not have time to contribute to the units package. Until this is fixed, I will continue to use plain vanilla positive real SymPy variables as units.

Regards
It's curious that this conversation has taken so long, when just 5 minutes of debugging have revealed this simple error:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/702bceaa0dde32193bfa9456df89eb63153a7538/sympy/physics/units/util.py#L33

`solve_least_squares` finds the solution to the matrix equation. In case no solution is found (like in `convert_to(joule*second, joule)`), it approximates to an inexact solution to the matrix system instead of raising an exception.

Simply changing it to `.solve_least_squares` to `.solve` should fix this issue.

problem_statement.Title: Symbol instances gain a per-instance __dict__ because Printable lacks __slots__
problem_statement.Description: After version 1.7, instances of Symbol (and other Basic-derived classes) have an instance __dict__ (e.g. sympy.Symbol('s').__dict__ exists) whereas in 1.6.2 they did not. This manifests as Symbol instances becoming dynamically extensible (an empty dict appears), contrary to previous behavior.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) sympy/core/_print_helpers.py: class Printable does not declare __slots__. Because sympy.core.basic.Basic inherits from Printable (class Basic(Printable, metaclass=ManagedProperties)), the absence of __slots__ on Printable causes Python to allocate a per-instance __dict__ for instances whose MRO includes Printable. 2) sympy/core/symbol.py: class Symbol declares __slots__ = ('name',) and its other ancestors (Atom, AtomicExpr, Boolean, Basic) declare either __slots__ = () or explicit __slots__ tuples (Basic.__slots__ = ('_mhash','_args','_assumptions')). The only class in the inspected MRO lacking a __slots__ declaration is Printable; therefore the presence of a per-instance __dict__ on Symbol instances is due to Printable not declaring __slots__. (The Python rule is: an instance will omit a per-instance __dict__ only if every class in the instance's MRO declares __slots__ and none includes '__dict__'.)
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python REPL with the affected SymPy version (>=1.7).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Symbol instances should not have a per-instance __dict__ by default (attempting to access __dict__ should raise AttributeError or __dict__ should not exist), matching the pre-1.7 behavior where Symbol instances were slot-only.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Symbol('s').__dict__ exists and returns an (empty) dict, indicating instances have a per-instance __dict__ and therefore accept arbitrary attribute assignment.

hints_text:
I've bisected the change to 5644df199fdac0b7a44e85c97faff58dfd462a5a from #19425
It seems that Basic now inherits `DefaultPrinting` which I guess doesn't have slots. I'm not sure if it's a good idea to add `__slots__` to that class as it would then affect all subclasses.

@eric-wieser 
I'm not sure if this should count as a regression but it's certainly not an intended change.
Maybe we should just get rid of `__slots__`. The benchmark results from #19425 don't show any regression from not using `__slots__`.
Adding `__slots__` won't affect subclasses - if a subclass does not specify `__slots__`, then the default is to add a `__dict__` anyway.

I think adding it should be fine.
Using slots can break multiple inheritance but only if the slots are non-empty I guess. Maybe this means that any mixin should always declare empty slots or it won't work properly with subclasses that have slots...

I see that `EvalfMixin` has `__slots__ = ()`.
I guess we should add empty slots to DefaultPrinting then. Probably the intention of using slots with Basic classes is to enforce immutability so this could be considered a regression in that sense so it should go into 1.7.1 I think.

problem_statement.Title: Pretty printer misrenders pi**(1/E) as an nth-root
problem_statement.Description: The Unicode/ASCII pretty printer renders pi**(1/E) using radical (nth-root) notation with an index taken from the exponent instead of showing the exponent (e^{-1} or exp(-1)). LaTeX and str representations are correct; the discrepancy is limited to the pretty printer output.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) PrettyPrinter._print_Pow (sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py) uses fraction(e) and then treats the pair (n, d) as indicating a 1/n exponent when n is S.One and d.is_Atom and not e.is_Integer. Concretely, the code does: n, d = fraction(e); if n is S.One and d.is_Atom and not e.is_Integer and self._settings['root_notation']: return self._print_nth_root(b, e). For e = 1/E, fraction(e) returns (1, E) so n is S.One and d.is_Atom is True; this causes the pretty printer to call _print_nth_root for a non-Rational exponent.

2) PrettyPrinter._print_nth_root (same file) is being called with non-Rational exponents. The helper's main logic expects a Rational exponent (it uses expt.q as the index when isinstance(expt, Rational)), and the fallback path for non-Rational exponents formats parts of the exponent object. Invoking this helper with symbolic or non-integer denominators (e.g. E) yields the incorrect radical rendering observed.

3) The correct diagnostic condition for deciding "root notation" (as used by the LaTeX printer) is: the exponent must be a true Rational with numerator ±1 and integer denominator > 1. Formally, the condition is e.is_Rational and abs(e.p) == 1 and e.q != 1 (see sympy/printing/latex.py). PrettyPrinter._print_Pow's fraction(...) + d.is_Atom test is too permissive and produces false positives such as 1/E.
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python session with SymPy imported (e.g. IPython).
from sympy import pi, E, latex
Evaluate pi**(1/E) at the interactive prompt (so the pretty printer is used); observe the pretty-printed output.
Compare with latex(pi**(1/E)) and str(pi**(1/E)) to confirm LaTeX/str show the exponent as e^{-1} / exp(-1).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The pretty printer should display pi**(1/E) with the exponent shown (equivalently to \pi^{e^{-1}} or 'pi**exp(-1)') — not as an nth-root. Only true rational exponents with numerator ±1 and integer denominator > 1 (e.g., Rational(1, n) or Rational(-1, n) with integer n>1) should be rendered using root notation.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The pretty printer renders pi**(1/E) using radical notation (nth-root), showing an exponent-derived index above the radical and π under the radical, while latex(pi**(1/E)) returns "\pi^{e^{-1}}" and str(pi**(1/E)) returns "pi**exp(-1)".

hints_text:
I can confirm this bug on master. Looks like it's been there a while
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/2d700c4b3c0871a26741456787b0555eed9d5546/sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py#L1814

`1/E` is `exp(-1)` which has totally different arg structure than something like `1/pi`:

```
>>> (1/E).args
(-1,)
>>> (1/pi).args
(pi, -1)
```
@ethankward nice!  Also, the use of `str` there isn't correct:
```
>>> pprint(7**(1/(pi)))                                                                                                                                                          
pi___
╲╱ 7 

>>> pprint(pi**(1/(pi)))                                                                                                                                                        
pi___
╲╱ π 

>>> pprint(pi**(1/(EulerGamma)))                                                                                                                                                
EulerGamma___
        ╲╱ π 
```
(`pi` and `EulerGamma` were not pretty printed)
I guess str is used because it's hard to put 2-D stuff in the corner of the radical like that. But I think it would be better to recursively call the pretty printer, and if it is multiline, or maybe even if it is a more complicated expression than just a single number or symbol name, then print it without the radical like

```
  1
  ─
  e
π
```

or

```
 ⎛ -1⎞
 ⎝e  ⎠
π

problem_statement.Title: refine() does not simplify arg(...) under assumptions (e.g. Q.positive), while Abs is simplified
problem_statement.Description: Calling refine(expr, assumptions) does not apply assumption-based simplifications to the argument/phase function arg(...). This appears when results contain arg(...) (for example inside a Piecewise returned by an integral) and boolean conditions that should become True/False under assumptions remain symbolic because arg(...) is not simplified by refine.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) refine dispatch uses two mechanisms (file: sympy/assumptions/refine.py): it (a) calls expr._eval_refine(assumptions) if that method exists (refine.py lines ~53-56) and otherwise (b) looks up a handler in handlers_dict by the expression's class name (refine.py lines ~57-61). handlers_dict (refine.py lines 376-384) contains entries for 'Abs','Pow','atan2','re','im','sign','MatrixElement' but does not contain an entry for 'arg'. Because 'arg' is not registered there, refine never dispatches a specialized handler for arg expressions.

2) The arg implementation (file: sympy/functions/elementary/complexes.py, class arg, lines ~700-771) does not implement a _eval_refine method. arg provides eval() and a _eval_rewrite_as_atan2 method (arg._eval_rewrite_as_atan2 returns atan2(im(z), re(z)) at complexes.py lines ~768-770), but without _eval_refine refine() cannot rely on a class-level refine hook for arg.

3) arg.eval only returns a numeric atan2 when the computed rv is numeric (complexes.py lines ~756-759 set rv = atan2(y,x) and return it only if rv.is_number). For symbolic inputs arg remains unevaluated rather than automatically rewritten to atan2. Combined with (1) and (2), there is no path inside refine() that reduces symbolic arg(...) to the canonical constants under assumptions.

4) The canonical principal-branch relationships that should allow refinement are not encoded for arg in refine: these are
   - arg(real > 0) == 0
   - arg(real < 0) == pi
   - arg(0) is undefined (NaN)
   - arg(pure_imag, imag > 0) == pi/2
   - arg(pure_imag, imag < 0) == -pi/2
   refine already encodes these canonical values for atan2 (see refine_atan2 in sympy/assumptions/refine.py, lines ~237-250), which explains why rewrite-to-atan2 plus a handler would allow these simplifications, but currently that path is not taken for symbolic arg(...).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Under appropriate assumptions refine should simplify arg(...) to the canonical principal-branch constants when determinable. Example: refine(arg(a), Q.positive(a)) should return 0. For the integral example, refine(J.doit(), Q.positive(a)) should simplify the Piecewise condition 2*Abs(arg(a)) < pi to True and return 1/(a**2 + 1).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: refine(J.doit(), Q.positive(a)) returns a Piecewise containing expressions with arg(a) still present (the condition 2*Abs(arg(a)) < pi is not reduced). refine(arg(a), Q.positive(a)) returns arg(a) unchanged. By contrast refine(abs(a), Q.positive(a)) correctly returns a.

problem_statement.Title: _print_SingularityFunction() TypeError in LaTeX printing when expression has exponent
problem_statement.Description: When printing a SingularityFunction-containing expression to LaTeX (for example, calling b2.shear_force() in a Jupyter notebook which triggers LaTeX repr), the LaTeX printer raises TypeError: _print_SingularityFunction() got an unexpected keyword argument 'exp'. The failure occurs in sympy/printing/latex.py while dispatching function printing.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) LatexPrinter._print_Function dispatches to function-specific handlers as handler(expr, exp). (Location: sympy/printing/latex.py, method: LatexPrinter._print_Function; see getattr(self, '_print_' + func)(expr, exp)).
2) LatexPrinter._print_Pow, when the base is a Function, calls self._print(expr.base, exp=self._print(expr.exp)), thereby causing handlers for functions to be invoked with an exp argument. (Location: sympy/printing/latex.py, method: LatexPrinter._print_Pow; see 'if expr.base.is_Function: return self._print(expr.base, exp=self._print(expr.exp))').
3) The LaTeX handler for SingularityFunction is defined as def _print_SingularityFunction(self, expr) and therefore does not accept an exp keyword argument. This signature-level mismatch causes Python to raise TypeError when the LaTeX printer passes exp. (Location: sympy/printing/latex.py, method: LatexPrinter._print_SingularityFunction; see 'def _print_SingularityFunction(self, expr):').
4) Conformance expectation: function-specific LaTeX handlers that may be called with an outer exponent by the LaTeX printer should accept an optional exp parameter (commonly exp=None) and, when exp is provided, parenthesize and apply the exponent to the rendered function. The latex printer provides a helper _do_exponent(expr, exp) for this purpose (Location: sympy/printing/latex.py, helper: LatexPrinter._do_exponent).
StepsToReproduce: Start a Python environment with SymPy available (e.g. Jupyter notebook).
Run the example from the original report:
E = symbols('E'); L = symbols('L'); F = symbols('F'); B, H = symbols('B, H')
Then evaluate: b2.shear_force()  # this triggers LaTeX printing and raises the TypeError
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: LaTeX rendering of expressions containing SingularityFunction (including when the SingularityFunction is part of a Pow) completes without raising TypeError and returns a LaTeX string that applies any outer exponent properly (parenthesized and with ^{...}).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling LaTeX printing (e.g. in Jupyter notebook output path) on an expression that includes SingularityFunction raises TypeError: _print_SingularityFunction() got an unexpected keyword argument 'exp', preventing LaTeX output and failing the cell display.

problem_statement.Title: PolynomialError from subs(): Piecewise gets detected as polynomial generator ("Piecewise generators do not make sense")
problem_statement.Description: Calling subs on expressions such as exp(sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x),(y, True)) / z)) can raise PolynomialError: "Piecewise generators do not make sense". The exception arises when SymPy's polification (conversion of subexpressions to polynomial form) is triggered during substitution/reconstruction. The occurrence depends on evaluation/assumption state (e.g. declaring x, y real) and on evaluation/caching order; under some states expr.subs({1: 1.0}) raises the PolynomialError while under others it succeeds.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/polys/polyutils.py::_parallel_dict_from_expr_no_gens — When no generators are supplied, the routine decomposes multiplicative factors into (base, exponent) and unconditionally adds the discovered 'base' into the generators set via gens.add(base). This implements the assumption that any multiplicative base encountered is a valid polynomial generator; as a result a Piecewise object that appears as a multiplicative base (e.g. Piecewise(...) in Piecewise(...)/z) can be added to the deduced gens set.

2) sympy/polys/polyutils.py::_parallel_dict_from_expr_if_gens — In the branch used when generators are provided, the routine attempts to map factor bases to provided generator indices and, on KeyError, treats factors whose free symbols do not intersect opt.gens as coefficients and otherwise raises PolynomialError("%s contains an element of the set of generators."). There is no special-case handling for Piecewise bases; a Piecewise base that is not an exact generator but whose free symbols intersect opt.gens will therefore cause this code path to treat the factor as containing a generator and raise, or otherwise be misclassified.

3) sympy/polys/polytools.py::_parallel_poly_from_expr — After generator detection, this routine explicitly rejects Piecewise objects in the deduced opt.gens, raising PolynomialError("Piecewise generators do not make sense"). This is the point where the observed exception is raised when a Piecewise was included in the generators set by the code above.

4) sympy/core/basic.py:Basic.subs / Basic._subs and class-specific _eval_subs implementations (e.g. Function._eval_subs, Piecewise._eval_subs) — The substitution machinery traverses and reconstructs subexpressions and calls class constructors (rv = self.func(*args)) during fallback reconstruction. That reconstruction/evaluation can cause polification routines to be invoked on sub-parts of the expression; when that happens and a Piecewise appears as a multiplicative base the generator-detection logic above may include or mis-handle the Piecewise, leading to the PolynomialError observed during expr.subs({1: 1.0}).
StepsToReproduce: python -c "from sympy import *; from sympy.core.cache import clear_cache; x,y,z = symbols('x y z'); clear_cache(); expr = exp(sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x),(y, True)) / z)); print(expr.subs({1: 1.0}))"  # works in some evaluation states

python -c "from sympy import *; from sympy.core.cache import clear_cache; x,y,z = symbols('x y z', real=True); clear_cache(); expr = exp(sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x),(y, True)) / z)); print(expr.subs({1: 1.0}))"  # reproduces PolynomialError: Piecewise generators do not make sense in the reported environment
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling subs({1: 1.0}) on the given expression should return the expression with integer 1 replaced by float 1.0 (no exception). The substitution should not raise a PolynomialError related to Piecewise being treated as a polynomial generator, regardless of symbol assumptions or caching/evaluation order.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When x and y are declared real (or under some evaluation/caching orders), expr.subs({1: 1.0}) raises PolynomialError with message: "Piecewise generators do not make sense". The error originates from SymPy's polynomial conversion pipeline after a Piecewise object was included in the deduced generator set.

The issue arises during a query in the old assumptions. The exponential function checks if its argument is zero here:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/624217179aaf8d094e6ff75b7493ad1ee47599b0/sympy/functions/elementary/exponential.py#L369
That gives:
```python
In [1]: x, y, z = symbols('x y z', real=True)

In [2]: sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x), (y, True)) * z**-1.0).is_zero
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
KeyError
```
Before processing the assumptions query the value of the queried assumption is stored as `None` here:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/624217179aaf8d094e6ff75b7493ad1ee47599b0/sympy/core/assumptions.py#L491-L493
That `None` remains there if an exception is raised during the query:
```python
In [1]: x, y, z = symbols('x y z', real=True)

In [2]: S = sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x), (y, True)) * z**-1.0)

In [3]: S._assumptions
Out[3]: {}

In [4]: try:
   ...:     S.is_zero
   ...: except Exception as e:
   ...:     print(e)
   ...: 
Piecewise generators do not make sense

In [5]: S._assumptions
Out[5]: 
{'zero': None,
 'extended_positive': None,
 'extended_real': None,
 'negative': None,
 'commutative': True,
 'extended_negative': None,
 'positive': None,
 'real': None}
```
A subsequent call to create the same expression returns the same object due to the cache and the object still has `None` is its assumptions dict:
```python
In [6]: S2 = sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x), (y, True)) * z**-1.0)

In [7]: S2 is S
Out[7]: True

In [8]: S2._assumptions
Out[8]: 
{'zero': None,
 'extended_positive': None,
 'extended_real': None,
 'negative': None,
 'commutative': True,
 'extended_negative': None,
 'positive': None,
 'real': None}

In [9]: S2.is_zero

In [10]: exp(sinh(Piecewise((x, y > x), (y, True)) * z**-1.0))
Out[10]: 
     ⎛ -1.0 ⎛⎧x  for x < y⎞⎞
 sinh⎜z    ⋅⎜⎨            ⎟⎟
     ⎝      ⎝⎩y  otherwise⎠⎠
ℯ  
```
Subsequent `is_zero` checks just return `None` from the assumptions dict without calling the handlers so they pass without raising.

The reason the `is_zero` handler raises first time around is due to the `sinh.is_real` handler which does this:
https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/624217179aaf8d094e6ff75b7493ad1ee47599b0/sympy/functions/elementary/hyperbolic.py#L250-L251
The `%` leads to `Mod` with the Piecewise which calls `gcd` as @jksuom showed above.

There are a few separate issues here:

1. The old assumptions system stores `None` when running a query but doesn't remove that `None` when an exception is raised.
2. `Mod` calls `gcd` on the argument when it is a Piecewise and `gcd` without catching the possible exception..
3. The `gcd` function raises an exception when given a `Piecewise`.

The fix suggested by @jksuom is for 2. which seems reasonable and I think we can merge a PR for that to fix using `Piecewise` with `Mod`.

I wonder about 3. as well though. Should `gcd` with a `Piecewise` raise an exception? If so then maybe `Mod` shouldn't be calling `gcd` at all. Perhaps just something like `gcd_terms` or `factor_terms` should be used there.

For point 1. I think that really the best solution is not putting `None` into the assumptions dict at all as there are other ways that it can lead to non-deterministic behaviour. Removing that line leads to a lot of different examples of RecursionError though (personally I consider each of those to be a bug in the old assumptions system).
I'll put a PR together. And, ah I see, yes you are right - good point (regarding TF float exponents).

I cannot comment on 1 as I'm not really familiar with the assumptions systems. But, regarding 3, would this exception make more sense as a `NotImplementedError` in `gcd`? Consider the potential behavior where `gcd` is applied to each condition of a `Piecewise` expression:

```python
In [1]: expr = Piecewise((x, x > 2), (2, True))

In [2]: expr
Out[2]: 
⎧x  for x > 2
⎨            
⎩2  otherwise

In [3]: gcd(x, x)
Out[3]: x

In [4]: gcd(2, x)
Out[4]: 1

In [5]: gcd(expr, x)  # current behavior
PolynomialError: Piecewise generators do not make sense

In [6]: gcd(expr, x)  # potential new behavior?
Out[6]: 
⎧x  for x > 2
⎨            
⎩1  otherwise
```

That would be what I expect from `gcd` here. For the `gcd` of two `Piecewise` expressions, this gets messier and I think would involve intersecting sets of conditions.

problem_statement.Title: LaTeX parser mis-parses nested \frac (missing grouping of fractional denominator)
problem_statement.Description: Parsing a LaTeX fraction whose denominator is itself a \frac produces an incorrectly grouped SymPy expression: the denominator is not treated as a single grouped unit, so the result shows an extra '/1/...' chain instead of dividing by the entire inner fraction.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The LaTeX parser constructs reciprocals by directly wrapping the parsed denominator expression in sympy.Pow(expr, -1, evaluate=False) without normalizing the internal structure of expr. This assumption appears in two places in sympy/parsing/latex/_parse_latex_antlr.py and is the root cause of the mis-grouping for nested fractions.

2) Specific locations that encode this assumption (both locations must be considered together and are equally responsible):
   - sympy/parsing/latex/_parse_latex_antlr.py:convert_frac — after converting the numerator and denominator, the code does:
       expr_top = convert_expr(frac.upper)
       expr_bot = convert_expr(frac.lower)
       inverse_denom = sympy.Pow(expr_bot, -1, evaluate=False)
     (see lines where inverse_denom is constructed and then used).

   - sympy/parsing/latex/_parse_latex_antlr.py:convert_mp — division is handled by constructing a multiplication by the reciprocal of the right-hand side via:
       return sympy.Mul(lh, sympy.Pow(rh, -1, evaluate=False), evaluate=False)
     (see the division branch that wraps the right-hand side in Pow(..., -1, evaluate=False)).

3) Consequence: when the denominator expression (expr_bot / rh) is itself a fraction or an unevaluated Pow/Mul that encodes a reciprocal, wrapping it in Pow(..., -1, evaluate=False) produces nested or multiple reciprocal factors that are left unevaluated. Because evaluate=False prevents automatic simplification/normalization, the resulting AST can contain nested Pow and Mul nodes that print or appear as a chain of divisions (for the reported example, producing ((a**3 + b)/c)/1/(c**2)) instead of representing the denominator as the grouped inner fraction (i.e., ((a**3 + b)/c)/(1/(c**2))).

4) The correct algebraic relationships that the parser's normalization would need to preserve (diagnostic statements of the invariant that is not being enforced):
   - inverse(Pow(base, exp)) should correspond to Pow(base, -exp) (i.e., 1/(base**exp) = base**(-exp)).
   - inverse(Mul(f1, f2, ...)) should correspond to Mul(inverse(f1), inverse(f2), ...) (i.e., 1/(a*b) = (1/a)*(1/b)), or otherwise be represented as a single grouped reciprocal when appropriate.

These incorrect assumptions and both listed code locations explain why nested \frac denominators are not preserved as grouped units in the constructed SymPy expression.
StepsToReproduce: Or in an interactive Python session:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The parser returns an expression where the outer fraction's denominator is the grouped inner fraction. For the example, the output should reflect ((a**3 + b)/c)/(1/(c**2)) — i.e., the denominator 1/(c**2) is treated as a single grouped unit.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The parser returns: ((a**3 + b)/c)/1/(c**2) — the denominator was not grouped, yielding a chain of divisions and a different structural expression than expected.

problem_statement.Title: Derivative.kind returns UndefinedKind instead of forwarding/inferencing matrix/array kinds
problem_statement.Description: An unevaluated Derivative does not report the mathematical 'kind' of the object it represents. For example, with A a MatrixSymbol, Integral(A, x).kind is MatrixKind(NumberKind) but Derivative(A, x).kind is UndefinedKind. The incorrect kind is observable on unevaluated Derivative objects (sympy.core.function.Derivative).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Basic.kind defaults to UndefinedKind (sympy/core/basic.py:110). Derivative (sympy/core/function.py: class Derivative) does not define or override a kind property and therefore inherits Basic.kind (UndefinedKind), so unevaluated Derivative(...).kind is UndefinedKind.

2) Integral obtains its kind by forwarding to its integrand via ExprWithLimits.kind (sympy/concrete/expr_with_limits.py:203-206) which returns self.function.kind; because MatrixSymbol / MatrixExpr provide a class-level kind (MatrixKind(NumberKind)), Integral(A, x).kind is MatrixKind(NumberKind). Derivative lacks this forwarding/inference, causing the observed divergence between Integral and Derivative behavior.

3) ArrayDerivative is created by the derivative dispatch when the expression or any differentiation variable is array-like (sympy/core/function.py:_derivative_dispatch; array types = MatrixCommon, MatrixExpr, NDimArray, list, tuple, Tuple) and ArrayDerivative subclasses Derivative (sympy/tensor/array/array_derivatives.py:12). Because Derivative currently has no kind inference, ArrayDerivative instances (unevaluated) also fall back to UndefinedKind in cases where the result is array-shaped (for example: differentiating a scalar with respect to a matrix yields an array-shaped derivative whose element-kind should be derived from the scalar's kind).

4) MatrixKind and ArrayKind encode element kinds via an element_kind parameter (sympy/matrices/common.py:3151.. and sympy/tensor/array/ndim_array.py:67..73). When a derivative is array-like the correct mathematical relationship is that the derivative's kind should be a MatrixKind or ArrayKind whose element_kind is derived from the expression's element kind (if the expression's kind is MatrixKind/ArrayKind) or from the expression's scalar kind (for scalar expressions). The current absence of Derivative.kind prevents this relationship from being represented on unevaluated Derivative/ArrayDerivative objects.
StepsToReproduce: from sympy import Derivative, Integral, MatrixSymbol
i = Integral(A, x); print(i.kind)  # prints MatrixKind(NumberKind)
d = Derivative(A, x); print(d.kind)  # prints UndefinedKind (incorrect)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The kind attribute of an unevaluated Derivative should reflect the mathematical object it represents: if the differentiated expression is matrix/array-like or any differentiation variable is matrix/array-like, Derivative.kind should be a MatrixKind or ArrayKind with an appropriate element_kind (derived from the expression's element kind or the expression's scalar kind). For purely scalar derivatives, Derivative.kind should match the expression's kind (e.g., NumberKind).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Derivative(A, x).kind returns UndefinedKind even when A is a MatrixSymbol (whose kind is MatrixKind(NumberKind)). Integral(A, x).kind correctly returns MatrixKind(NumberKind).

As I dig deeper into this issue, the problem is much larger than `Derivative`. As a matter of facts, all functions should be able to deal with `kind`. At the moment:

```
from sympy import MatrixSymbol
A = MatrixSymbol('A', 2, 2)
sin(A).kind
# UndefinedKind
```
The kind attribute is new and is not fully implemented or used across the codebase.

For `sin` and other functions I don't think that we should allow the ordinary `sin` function to be used for the Matrix sin. There should be a separate `MatrixSin` function for that.

For Derivative the handler for kind just needs to be added.

problem_statement.Title: RecursionError evaluating .is_zero on cosh(acos(-i + acosh(-g + i)))
problem_statement.Description: Accessing the .is_zero property on the expression cosh(acos(-i + acosh(-g + i))) triggers a RecursionError (maximum recursion depth exceeded). The failure occurs during SymPy's assumption evaluation (is_zero / is_extended_real) while evaluating predicates on Add/Mul/Function subexpressions, not during parsing or construction of the expression.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/core/add.py — Add._eval_is_imaginary: this method inspects terms to determine whether they contribute to the imaginary part. In its logic it sometimes constructs a temporary product (S.ImaginaryUnit * a) and queries (S.ImaginaryUnit*a).is_extended_real (see sympy/core/add.py lines 667-670). Materializing that Mul triggers Mul's assumption-evaluation machinery (Mul._eval_is_extended_real -> Mul._eval_real_imag), which iterates over the Mul's factors and queries properties of the original term (e.g. t.is_imaginary, t.is_extended_real, t.is_zero; see sympy/core/mul.py lines 1380-1396). Those back-and-forth queries can re-enter Add's assumption logic for the original term and, in nested expressions, cause re-entrant evaluation that overflows the Python recursion limit.

2) sympy/core/add.py — Add._eval_is_zero: similar pattern is present here. Add._eval_is_zero constructs (S.ImaginaryUnit * a) and queries its is_extended_real to set an im_or_z flag used in the zero test (see sympy/core/add.py lines 696-699). As above, creating that Mul invokes Mul's evaluator which queries properties on 'a' and can cause mutual recursion between assumption evaluators.

3) Logical-equivalence observation (diagnostic): the predicate being tested by these Mul constructions is logically the primitive predicate 'a.is_imaginary' because multiplying a purely imaginary quantity by I yields a real quantity. Concretely, (I*a).is_extended_real is true exactly when 'a' is purely imaginary (with the surrounding Add logic already handling explicit zero checks). This equivalence explains why the code is asking (I*a).is_extended_real, and also shows that the behavior can be determined without constructing a Mul (the equivalence itself is a diagnostic observation, not a prescription).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Querying expr.is_zero should return True, False, or None to indicate whether the expression is zero (or indeterminate), and must not raise a RecursionError.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Evaluating expr.is_zero raises RecursionError: maximum recursion depth exceeded while calling a Python object (assumption evaluation re-enters itself and overflows recursion).

 We should probably never come to this test when the argument is real. There should be something like `if arg.is_extended_real` before `conj` is computed.
There are tests for nonnegative, nonpositive and imaginary. So an additional test before coming to this part would be
```python
if arg.is_extended_real:
    return
...
_arg = signsimp(arg, evaluate=False)
if _arg not in (conj, -conj):
...
```

problem_statement.Title: itermonomials omits mixed monomials when min_degrees is used (total-degree mode)
problem_statement.Description: When itermonomials is called with integer max_degrees and min_degrees (total-degree mode), it filters candidate monomials using the maximum single-variable exponent instead of the total degree, causing mixed monomials of the correct total degree to be omitted.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) In the total-degree mode of sympy/polys/monomials.py::itermonomials the degree test is incorrect: the function tests max(powers.values()) >= min_degree (uses the largest single exponent) instead of testing the total degree. The total degree of a monomial is the sum of its exponents: total_degree(monom) == sum(exponents). Location: sympy/polys/monomials.py, itermonomials, commutative branch (the if using combinations_with_replacement). 2) The same incorrect test appears in the non-commutative total-degree branch of itermonomials. Location: sympy/polys/monomials.py, itermonomials, non-commutative branch (the if using product with repeat=max_degree). 3) The per-variable-degree (list) branch of itermonomials is implemented differently (constructs explicit per-variable power lists and uses product(*power_lists)) and does not contain the max(powers.values()) test; it is not affected. (All three findings have equal weight and are located in sympy/polys/monomials.py as noted above.)
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL or create a script using SymPy.
Run: import sympy as sp; from sympy.polys.orderings import monomial_key
Define: x1, x2, x3 = sp.symbols('x1, x2, x3'); states = [x1, x2, x3]; max_degrees = 3; min_degrees = 3
Run: monomials = sorted(sp.itermonomials(states, max_degrees, min_degrees=min_degrees), key=monomial_key('grlex', states)); print(monomials)
Observe the returned list of monomials.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generator should return all monomials monom in the given variables for which min_degree <= total_degree(mon) <= max_degree, including mixed monomials of the same total degree (e.g., for degree 3 and variables x1,x2,x3 it should include x1*x2**2, x1*x3**2, x1**2*x2, etc., in addition to x1**3, x2**3, x3**3).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: For the example with max_degrees = 3 and min_degrees = 3 the output is only [x3**3, x2**3, x1**3]; mixed monomials whose total degree equals 3 (such as x1*x2**2) are missing.

problem_statement.Title: solve_poly_system returns 1-tuple for positive-dimensional system when Groebner basis is a single univariate polynomial
problem_statement.Description: When solve_poly_system is called with multiple generators but the computed Groebner basis contains a single univariate polynomial (for example, solve_poly_system((y - 1,), x, y)), the solver returns a list of 1-tuples ([(1,)]) instead of signalling that the solution set is positive-dimensional (infinitely many solutions). This yields an incorrect finite representation for a system with free variables.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) The univariate detection path is taken without checking the number of requested generators. Location: sympy/solvers/polysys.py, nested function _solve_reduced_system (inside solve_generic). The code filters a Groebner basis for univariate elements and binds the polynomial with the condition

    univariate = list(filter(_is_univariate, basis))

    if len(univariate) == 1:
        f = univariate.pop()

(lines ~241-245)

This condition does not require that len(gens) == 1, so a single univariate basis polynomial may be accepted even when multiple generators were requested.

2) An early return produces single-element tuples even when other variables remain free. Location: same function. After computing zeros for the univariate polynomial the code does:

    zeros = list(roots(f.ltrim(gen)).keys())
    if not zeros:
        return []
    if len(basis) == 1:
        return [(zero,) for zero in zeros]

(lines ~254-260)

When len(basis) == 1 this returns 1-tuples ([(zero,) ...]) regardless of the number of requested generators. If len(gens) > 1 this yields incomplete solution tuples and an incorrect finite listing for a positive-dimensional system.

Note: there is a separate base-case earlier that correctly returns 1-tuples when the input system and gens are both of length 1:

    if len(system) == len(gens) == 1:
        zeros = list(roots(system[0], gens[-1]).keys())
        return [(zero,) for zero in zeros]

(lines ~227-231). That base-case is valid; the issue is specifically the later early-return when the Groebner basis collapses to one polynomial while multiple gens were requested.

3) The early-return bypasses the later guard that would detect tuple-length mismatches. Location: same function. After recursively constructing solutions the function checks:

    if solutions and len(solutions[0]) != len(gens):
        raise NotImplementedError(...)

(lines ~277-281)

However, the early return at len(basis) == 1 exits before this guard is reached, so incomplete 1-tuples can be returned without triggering the intended NotImplementedError for positive-dimensional systems.

Summary diagnostic relationship (explicit logical condition encoded incorrectly): the single-univariate handling is only safe when there is exactly one requested generator. In logical terms the single-univariate path that returns 1-tuples is only valid when len(univariate) == 1 AND len(gens) == 1. The code currently requires only len(univariate) == 1.
StepsToReproduce: In a Python REPL or script: from sympy import symbols; from sympy.solvers import solve_poly_system; x, y = symbols('x y')
Call solve_poly_system with a one-variable polynomial but two generators: solve_poly_system((y - 1,), x, y)
Compare with solve_poly_system((x - 1,), x, y) to observe differing behavior
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: For a system that is not zero-dimensional (has free variables / infinitely many solutions) the solver should indicate that only zero-dimensional systems are supported by raising NotImplementedError with a message such as "only zero-dimensional systems supported (finite number of solutions)". For truly zero-dimensional systems the solver should return a list of solution tuples whose length equals the number of requested generators.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling solve_poly_system((y - 1,), x, y) returns [(1,)] (a list of 1-tuples), i.e. a partial/incomplete solution tuple instead of raising NotImplementedError. By contrast solve_poly_system((x - 1,), x, y) raises NotImplementedError; the behavior is inconsistent and the returned 1-tuple incorrectly represents a finite solution set when the system is positive-dimensional.

problem_statement.Title: sp.S('Point2D(...)') raises "Imaginary coordinates are not permitted." when called inside with sp.evaluate(False)
problem_statement.Description: Calling sympify on a string that constructs a Point2D inside a with sp.evaluate(False) context raises ValueError('Imaginary coordinates are not permitted.') even for purely real numeric coordinates.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1. Point.__new__ performs a boolean test that relies on im(a) evaluating to a falsy numeric zero for real numeric coordinates. Location: sympy/geometry/point.py, Point.__new__. Evidence: the check is written as "if any(a.is_number and im(a) for a in coords):" and raises ValueError when the expression is truthy.

2. parse_expr(..., evaluate=False) transforms calls to certain functions (including im) by appending evaluate=False to the call node in the AST. Location: sympy/parsing/sympy_parser.py, EvaluateFalseTransformer (functions tuple and visit_Call). Evidence: the transformer lists 'im' in its functions tuple and visit_Call appends the keyword evaluate=False to those calls.

3. Applied function construction respects the evaluate keyword: when evaluate=False is passed into an applied function (e.g. im(..., evaluate=False)), the Function/Application constructor does not call the function's eval() and instead returns an unevaluated Application object. Location: sympy/core/function.py, Application.__new__ (handling of the evaluate option).

4. Unevaluated Application/Function objects are truthy in Python while numeric zeros are falsy. Interaction of (1)-(3) produces a false positive: when parse_expr(..., evaluate=False) leaves im(a) unevaluated, the expression im(a) in the generator yields a truthy object and the Point.__new__ boolean test incorrectly treats a real numeric coordinate as having an imaginary part. Locations: sympy/core/basic.py (Basic has no __bool__), sympy/core/numbers.py (number __bool__ returns False for zero).

5. Diagnostic/formula (observed-by-inspection): for numeric coordinates 'a' the canonical way to detect a non-real numeric is to test the number's realness property (e.g. check that a.is_real is False) rather than rely on the truthiness of im(a).
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import sympy as sp; with sp.evaluate(False): sp.S('Point2D(Integer(1),Integer(2))')"
Alternatively, in an interactive Python session run:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Sympifying the string 'Point2D(Integer(1),Integer(2))' inside or outside an evaluate(False) context returns a Point2D object with the given real integer coordinates and does not raise an exception.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When sympifying the same string under with sp.evaluate(False), sympy raises ValueError: 'Imaginary coordinates are not permitted.' even though the coordinates are real integers.

problem_statement.Title: cse() creates redundant matrix copies when MatrixExpr elements are also eliminated
problem_statement.Description: Common-subexpression-elimination (cse) can produce replacements that include both whole MatrixExpr objects (e.g., MatrixSymbol) and their scalar MatrixElement children. This yields redundant temporaries and elementwise copies when generating code for MatrixExprs (for example, a full matrix copy x0 := a plus scalar temporaries x1 := x0[0,0]). The issue arises when both the matrix node and its element nodes are independently selected as common subexpressions.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) tree_cse identifies repeated subexpressions independently and adds each repeated node to the to_eliminate set. It does not exclude MatrixElement nodes whose parent MatrixExpr is also in to_eliminate. Location: sympy/simplify/cse_main.py, function tree_cse (the _find_repeated collection and the to_eliminate set). Effect: to_eliminate may contain both a MatrixExpr (for example, a) and MatrixElement(a, i, j), which results in separate replacement entries for the matrix and for its elements.

2) When tree_cse creates a replacement for a whole-matrix subexpression (orig_expr that is a MatrixExpr), it converts the generated scalar symbol into a MatrixSymbol with the same shape using the pattern sym = MatrixSymbol(sym.name, orig_expr.rows, orig_expr.cols). Location: sympy/simplify/cse_main.py, tree_cse/_rebuild (the MatrixExpr handling when orig_expr is in to_eliminate). Effect: replacements include LHS entries that are MatrixSymbol('xN', rows, cols) for whole-matrix temporaries.

3) MatrixElement and single-index handling use row-major flattening for element access. Locations: sympy/matrices/expressions/matexpr.py and sympy/printing/c.py. Exact relationships: MatrixExpr single-index access computes i = key // cols and j = key % cols (matexpr.py); the C printer prints MatrixElement(parent, i, j) as parent[j + i*parent.shape[1]] (printing/c.py). Effect: when both (x0, a) and element-level replacements referencing x0 appear, code generation emits elementwise assignments of the matrix (x0[...] = a[...]) and also scalar assignments reading from x0's elements (x1 = x0[...]), producing redundant copies and temporaries.

(Each numbered item above is an independent encoded assumption or relationship that contributes to the observed behaviour; this pattern applies generally to MatrixExpr / MatrixElement pairs, not only to MatrixSymbol.)
StepsToReproduce: Create 4x4 MatrixSymbols a and b and a matrix-of-symbols helper (as in the original report).
Construct expression e = a*b (as Matrix of symbol entries) and substitute MatrixSymbols into that expression so the expression uses MatrixSymbol('a',4,4) and MatrixSymbol('b',4,4).
Call sympy.cse on the substituted expression (cse_subs, cse_reduced = sympy.cse(e2)) and inspect the replacement list cse_subs and/or run C code generation on the replacements.

Observe that replacements include both whole-matrix entries such as (x0, a) and element-level replacements such as (x1, x0[0,0]) and that the generated C code contains both elementwise matrix copies (x0[...] = a[...];) and scalar assignments from x0's elements.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: cse should not produce redundant element-level temporaries that reference newly created whole-matrix temporaries. If a whole MatrixExpr (a matrix) is chosen as a common subexpression and replaced by a matrix temporary, element accesses should refer directly to that matrix temporary (e.g., x0[i, j]) rather than producing additional scalar temporaries that read from the matrix temporary. User-visible result: the replacement list would contain matrix-level temporaries (e.g., (x0, a), (x2, b)) and the reduced expression should refer to x0[i,j] and x2[i,j] directly without extra scalar temporaries defined as x1 := x0[i,j].
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: cse produced replacements that include whole-matrix temporaries and also scalar temporaries referencing those matrix temporaries. Example output shows replacements like (x0, a), (x1, x0[0,0]), (x2, b), (x3, x2[0,0]), etc., and generated C code that first assigns each element of x0 from a (x0[0] = a[0]; ... x0[15] = a[15];) and then assigns scalar temporaries from x0's elements (x1 = x0[0]; ...). This results in redundant copies and extra temporaries.

result[11] = a[8]*b[3] + a[9]*b[7] + a[10]*b[11] + a[11]*b[15];
result[12] = a[12]*b[0] + a[13]*b[4] + a[14]*b[8] + a[15]*b[12];
result[13] = a[12]*b[1] + a[13]*b[5] + a[14]*b[9] + a[15]*b[13];
result[14] = a[12]*b[2] + a[13]*b[6] + a[14]*b[10] + a[15]*b[14];
result[15] = a[12]*b[3] + a[13]*b[7] + a[14]*b[11] + a[15]*b[15];
```
Thanks. Note that it doesn't look like cse is well tested (i.e. designed) for MatrixSymbols based on the unit tests: https://github.com/sympy/sympy/blob/master/sympy/simplify/tests/test_cse.py#L315. Those tests don't really prove that it works as desired. So this definitely needs to be fixed.
The first part works as expected:

```
In [1]: import sympy as sm

In [2]: M = sm.MatrixSymbol('M', 3, 3)

In [3]: B = sm.MatrixSymbol('B', 3, 3)

In [4]: M * B
Out[4]: M*B

In [5]: sm.cse(M * B)
Out[5]: ([], [M*B])
```
For the ccode of an expression of MatrixSymbols, I would not expect it to print the results as you have them. MatrixSymbols should map to a matrix algebra library like BLAS and LINPACK. But Matrix, on the other hand, should do what you expect. Note how this works:

```
In [8]: M = sm.Matrix(3, 3, lambda i, j: sm.Symbol('M_{}{}'.format(i, j)))

In [9]: M
Out[9]: 
Matrix([
[M_00, M_01, M_02],
[M_10, M_11, M_12],
[M_20, M_21, M_22]])

In [10]: B = sm.Matrix(3, 3, lambda i, j: sm.Symbol('B_{}{}'.format(i, j)))

In [11]: B
Out[11]: 
Matrix([
[B_00, B_01, B_02],
[B_10, B_11, B_12],
[B_20, B_21, B_22]])

In [12]: M * B
Out[12]: 
Matrix([
[B_00*M_00 + B_10*M_01 + B_20*M_02, B_01*M_00 + B_11*M_01 + B_21*M_02, B_02*M_00 + B_12*M_01 + B_22*M_02],
[B_00*M_10 + B_10*M_11 + B_20*M_12, B_01*M_10 + B_11*M_11 + B_21*M_12, B_02*M_10 + B_12*M_11 + B_22*M_12],
[B_00*M_20 + B_10*M_21 + B_20*M_22, B_01*M_20 + B_11*M_21 + B_21*M_22, B_02*M_20 + B_12*M_21 + B_22*M_22]])

In [13]: sm.cse(M * B)
Out[13]: 
([], [Matrix([
  [B_00*M_00 + B_10*M_01 + B_20*M_02, B_01*M_00 + B_11*M_01 + B_21*M_02, B_02*M_00 + B_12*M_01 + B_22*M_02],
  [B_00*M_10 + B_10*M_11 + B_20*M_12, B_01*M_10 + B_11*M_11 + B_21*M_12, B_02*M_10 + B_12*M_11 + B_22*M_12],
  [B_00*M_20 + B_10*M_21 + B_20*M_22, B_01*M_20 + B_11*M_21 + B_21*M_22, B_02*M_20 + B_12*M_21 + B_22*M_22]])])

In [17]: print(sm.ccode(M * B, assign_to=sm.MatrixSymbol('E', 3, 3)))
E[0] = B_00*M_00 + B_10*M_01 + B_20*M_02;
E[1] = B_01*M_00 + B_11*M_01 + B_21*M_02;
E[2] = B_02*M_00 + B_12*M_01 + B_22*M_02;
E[3] = B_00*M_10 + B_10*M_11 + B_20*M_12;
E[4] = B_01*M_10 + B_11*M_11 + B_21*M_12;
E[5] = B_02*M_10 + B_12*M_11 + B_22*M_12;
E[6] = B_00*M_20 + B_10*M_21 + B_20*M_22;
E[7] = B_01*M_20 + B_11*M_21 + B_21*M_22;
E[8] = B_02*M_20 + B_12*M_21 + B_22*M_22;
```
But in order to get a single input argument from codegen it cannot be different symbols, and if you replace each symbol with a `MatrixSymbol[i, j]` then `cse()` starts doing the above non-optiimizations for some reason.
As far as I know, `codegen` does not work with Matrix or MatrixSymbol's in any meaningful way. There are related issues:

#11456
#4367
#10522

In general, there needs to be work done in the code generators to properly support matrices.

As a work around, I suggest using `ccode` and a custom template to get the result you want.

problem_statement.Title: Array([]) construction raises ValueError while Matrix([]) works
problem_statement.Description: Constructing an Array from an empty Python iterable (e.g. Array([])) raises a ValueError during shape inference; Matrix([]) does not fail. The failure occurs during recursive shape scanning of the top-level iterable.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) NDimArray._scan_iterable_shape (sympy/tensor/array/ndim_array.py) assumes any object detected as Iterable contains at least one element. The implementation performs elems, shapes = zip(*[f(i) for i in pointer]) on the iterable; when pointer is empty this expands to zip(*) on an empty list and raises ValueError("not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 0)").

2) NDimArray._handle_ndarray_creation_inputs (sympy/tensor/array/ndim_array.py) calls _scan_iterable_shape whenever an Iterable is provided and shape is None; therefore the scanner failure prevents array construction early in the creation pipeline.

3) The dense and sparse array constructors rely on the (shape, flat_list) semantics produced by the handler and on downstream invariants:
   - ImmutableDenseNDimArray._new (sympy/tensor/array/dense_ndim_array.py) calls _handle_ndarray_creation_inputs and then expects the returned shape and flat_list; it computes _loop_size with functools.reduce(lambda x,y: x*y, shape, 1).
   - MutableDenseNDimArray._new (sympy/tensor/array/dense_ndim_array.py) calls _handle_ndarray_creation_inputs and computes _loop_size with functools.reduce(lambda x,y: x*y, shape) if shape else len(flat_list).
   - ImmutableSparseNDimArray._new and MutableSparseNDimArray._new (sympy/tensor/array/sparse_ndim_array.py) also call _handle_ndarray_creation_inputs and rely on the same shape/flat_list conventions.

4) The codebase already encodes the canonical representation for an empty top-level iterable: shape == (0,) and flat_list == []. _check_special_bounds (sympy/tensor/array/ndim_array.py) enforces that shape == () corresponds to exactly one scalar element and that shape == (0,) corresponds to zero elements. Representing an empty top-level iterable as flat_list = [] and shape = (0,) is consistent with these checks and with subsequent computations (e.g. _loop_size reduction yielding 0 for shapes containing 0).
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import sympy; print(sympy.__version__)"  # shows '1.4' in the report's environment
Expected traceback (observed): ValueError: not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 0) with stack trace into sympy/tensor/array/ndim_array.py::_scan_iterable_shape
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Constructing an Array from an empty Python iterable should succeed and produce an empty 1-D Array. In user-visible terms: Array([]) should be a valid Array with shape (0,), length 0, iteration producing no elements, and tolist() returning [].
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Array([]) raises ValueError during shape inference with message: "not enough values to unpack (expected 2, got 0)" and a stack trace inside sympy/tensor/array/ndim_array.py (NDimArray._scan_iterable_shape). Matrix([]) constructs successfully.

hints_text: Technically, `Array([], shape=(0,))` works. It is just unable to understand the shape of `[]`.

--- a/sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py

@@ -1144,22 +1144,24 @@ def _print_BasisDependent(self, expr):
             if '\n' in partstr:
                 tempstr = partstr
                 tempstr = tempstr.replace(vectstrs[i], '')
-                if '\N{right parenthesis extension}' in tempstr:   # If scalar is a fraction
+                if '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS EXTENSION}' in tempstr:   # If scalar is a fraction
                     for paren in range(len(tempstr)):
                         flag[i] = 1
-                        if tempstr[paren] == '\N{right parenthesis extension}':
-                            tempstr = tempstr[:paren] + '\N{right parenthesis extension}'\
+                        if tempstr[paren] == '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS EXTENSION}' and tempstr[paren + 1] == '\n':
+                            # We want to place the vector string after all the right parentheses, because
+                            # otherwise, the vector will be in the middle of the string
+                            tempstr = tempstr[:paren] + '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS EXTENSION}'\
                                          + ' '  + vectstrs[i] + tempstr[paren + 1:]
                             break
                 elif '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS LOWER HOOK}' in tempstr:
-                    flag[i] = 1
-                    tempstr = tempstr.replace('\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS LOWER HOOK}',
-                                        '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS LOWER HOOK}'
-                                        + ' ' + vectstrs[i])
-                else:
-                    tempstr = tempstr.replace('\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS UPPER HOOK}',
-                                        '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS UPPER HOOK}'
-                                        + ' ' + vectstrs[i])
+                    # We want to place the vector string after all the right parentheses, because
+                    # otherwise, the vector will be in the middle of the string. For this reason,
+                    # we insert the vector string at the rightmost index.
+                    index = tempstr.rfind('\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS LOWER HOOK}')
+                    if index != -1: # then this character was found in this string
+                        flag[i] = 1
+                        tempstr = tempstr[:index] + '\N{RIGHT PARENTHESIS LOWER HOOK}'\
+                                     + ' '  + vectstrs[i] + tempstr[index + 1:]
                 o1[i] = tempstr
 
         o1 = [x.split('\n') for x in o1]

problem_statement.Title: Unicode pretty-print: unit vector label inserted into multiline scalar when pretty-printing sympy.vector (BasisDependent) objects
problem_statement.Description: When pretty-printing sympy.vector (BasisDependent) objects in a terminal using the Unicode pretty-printer, the unit-vector label (e.g. i_C, j_C, k_C) can be inserted into the middle of a multiline pretty-printed scalar coefficient instead of being placed at the end of the corresponding term. The output becomes visually jumbled and misaligned.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Fragile string-level assembly in PrettyPrinter._print_BasisDependent (sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py). The method converts the prettyForm returned for a coefficient into a raw string via self._print(v).parens()[0] and then concatenates and manipulates those raw strings together with unit-vector name substrings. (See e.g. arg_str = self._print(v).parens()[0] and o1.append(arg_str + ' ' + k._pretty_form').)

2) Reliance on literal unit-vector name substrings produced by vector classes and textual replace operations. The pretty-printer collects k._pretty_form (plain strings like 'i_C') and later calls tempstr.replace(vectstrs[i], '') to remove/reposition those substrings in raw strings. That logic assumes the vector label always appears as a removable substring in the coefficient's raw string, which is not guaranteed for multiline prettyForms.

3) Heuristic Unicode-character checks used during textual insertion. The code inspects specific Unicode characters (e.g. 'right parenthesis extension', 'RIGHT PARENTHESIS LOWER HOOK', 'RIGHT PARENTHESIS UPPER HOOK') as a heuristic to decide where to re-insert vector labels in the raw string. Those character-based heuristics are brittle when applied to multiline prettyForms and can cause labels to be placed inside the scalar block.

4) Mixing prettyForm/stringPict structured primitives with raw string operations. prettyForm / stringPict objects carry baseline/width/multiline layout information; extracting the raw string (parens()[0]) and performing substring operations discards that structure and undermines the pretty-printer's layout invariants. The affected code wraps the final joined raw string back into a prettyForm at the end, losing the original structured layout information.

Equal-weighted related locations (context):
- Pretty-printer assembly: sympy/printing/pretty/pretty.py::PrettyPrinter._print_BasisDependent (the site of the fragile string manipulation).
- Sources of plain-string labels used by the printer: sympy/vector/vector.py::BaseVector (obj._pretty_form assigned as a plain string), sympy/vector/scalar.py::BaseScalar (obj._pretty_form assigned and BaseScalar._pretty returns prettyForm(self._pretty_form)), and sympy/vector/dyadic.py::BaseDyadic (obj._pretty_form formed by concatenating vector component _pretty_form strings). These class-level plain-string labels are consumed by the pretty-printer as substrings in the fragile assembly.
StepsToReproduce: Create a Python file or interactive session with SymPy imported.

Run the following code (exactly as shown):

from sympy import *
from sympy.vector import CoordSys3D, Del

init_printing()

delop = Del()
CC_ = CoordSys3D("C")
x,    y,    z    = CC_.x, CC_.y, CC_.z
xhat, yhat, zhat = CC_.i, CC_.j, CC_.k

t = symbols("t")
ten = symbols("10", positive=True)
eps, mu = 4*pi*ten**(-11), ten**(-5)

Bx = 2 * ten**(-4) * cos(ten**5 * t) * sin(ten**(-3) * y)
vecB = Bx * xhat
vecE = (1/eps) * Integral(delop.cross(vecB/mu).doit(), t)

pprint(vecB)
print()
pprint(vecE)
print()
pprint(vecE.doit())
Observe the printed output in the terminal: unit-vector labels (e.g. i_C, k_C) are placed inside or between lines of a multiline coefficient instead of appearing at the end of their term.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Multiline pretty-printed vector expressions should show each term's scalar coefficient as a coherent block, with the unit-vector label (i_C, j_C, k_C) visually aligned to the right (at the end) of that block. No unit-vector label should be inserted into the middle of a multiline scalar representation; the multiline layout and label placement should remain intact.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: In the terminal Unicode pretty-print output, the unit-vector label (e.g. i_C, k_C) appears inserted into the middle of a multiline scalar pretty-printed block, breaking the visual layout and producing a jumbled/misaligned appearance (as shown in the example where the unit vector label is intermixed with the lines of an integral/coefficient).

hints_text:
You can control print order as described [here](https://stackoverflow.com/a/58541713/1089161).
The default order should not break the multiline bracket of pretty print. Please see the output in constant width mode or paste it in a text editor. The second output is fine while the right bracket is broken in the other two.
I can verify that this seems to be an issue specific to pretty print. The Latex renderer outputs what you want. This should be fixable. Here is an image of the output for your vectors from the latex rendered in Jupyter.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1231317/153658279-1cf4d387-2101-4cb3-b182-131ed3cbe1b8.png)

Admittedly the small outer parenthesis are not stylistically great, but the ordering is what you expect.
The LaTeX printer ought to be using \left and \right for parentheses.

problem_statement.Title: lambdify generates incorrect code for singleton Python tuples (missing trailing comma)
problem_statement.Description: When lambdify generates Python source for an expression that is a native 1-tuple (e.g., tuple([1]) or (1,)), the emitted code uses parentheses without the required trailing comma ("(1)"), so the generated function returns an int instead of a 1-tuple.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import inspect; from sympy import lambdify; print(inspect.getsource(lambdify([], tuple([1]))))"
python -c "import inspect; from sympy import lambdify; print(inspect.getsource(lambdify([], tuple([1,2]))))"
Optionally, create a lambdified function and inspect its runtime return type: python -c "from sympy import lambdify; f = lambdify([], tuple([1])); print(type(f()), f())"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The generated function source for a native one-element tuple should include the trailing comma so that the function returns a tuple. For the MWE, inspect.getsource(lambdify([], tuple([1]))) should contain: 'return (1,)' (and the runtime value of calling the function should be (1,)).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The generated source contains 'return (1)' (missing the trailing comma), so calling the generated function yields an int (1) instead of the expected tuple (1,). For multi-element tuples (e.g. tuple([1, 2])) the generated code is correct ('return (1, 2)').

hints_text:
Bisected to 6ccd2b07ded5074941bb80b5967d60fa1593007a from #21993.

CC @bjodah 
As a work around for now, you can use the `Tuple` object from sympy. Note that it is constructed slightly differently from a python `tuple`, rather than giving a `list`, you give it multiple input arguments (or you can put a `*` in front of your list):
```python
>>> inspect.getsource(lambdify([], Tuple(*[1])))
def _lambdifygenerated():\n    return (1,)\n
>>> inspect.getsource(lambdify([], Tuple(1)))
def _lambdifygenerated():\n    return (1,)\n
```
Of course the problem should also be fixed. `lambdify` is in a bit of an awkward spot, it supports a lot of different input and output formats that make it practically impossible to keep any functionality that is not explicitly tested for whenever you make a change.



> As a work around for now, you can use the `Tuple` object from sympy. Note that it is constructed slightly differently from a python `tuple`, rather than giving a `list`, you give it multiple input arguments (or you can put a `*` in front of your list):

Thank you! This is tested to be working in SymPy 1.6-1.10. Consider this issue addressed for now. 

`lambdify` (or generally, the code generation) is an extremely useful tool. Are you aware of any roadmap or discussions on the refactoring of `lambdify` (or codegen)? I would like to contribute to it. 

I want to put out a 1.10.1 bugfix release. Should this be fixed?

problem_statement.Title: UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension miscomputes Function result dimension (exp) causing ValueError
problem_statement.Description: When evaluating expressions containing functions (e.g. exp) applied to a unit expression that is dimensionless, UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension computes incorrect dimensional information for the Function node. As a result, expressions like 100 + exp(second/(ohm*farad)) cause a dimension mismatch error: the Function node is reported with the argument's Dimension (time/(capacitance*impedance)) rather than the function result's Dimension (dimensionless), producing a ValueError when combined with a numeric literal.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension (sympy/physics/units/unitsystem.py) incorrectly handles Function nodes: it collects (factor, dimension) for each argument and returns (expr.func(*(factors)), *(d[1] for each arg)). Concretely, see lines 192-196 where the Function branch returns expr.func applied to argument factors and then returns the argument dimension(s) instead of a single Dimension for the function result. For single-argument functions this yields the argument's Dimension as the function result Dimension; for multi-argument functions it can return more than two tuple elements. This is inconsistent with the method's documented intent to return a two-tuple (scale factor expression, dimension) and causes downstream dimension equality checks to compare against the wrong Dimension.

2) Quantity._collect_factor_and_dimension (sympy/physics/units/quantities.py) is a deprecated wrapper that delegates to UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension (see lines 198-211). Because it delegates, the incorrect behavior in UnitSystem propagates to the Quantity API surface.

3) Inconsistency with UnitSystem.get_dimensional_expr (sympy/physics/units/unitsystem.py): get_dimensional_expr already treats a function result as S.One when all argument dimensional expressions equal 1 (see lines 143-147). _collect_factor_and_dimension does not mirror that semantic — instead it returns argument Dimensions — creating a conflicting assumption about Function node semantics. DimensionSystem enforces function-dimension rules and recognizes function results as dimensionless when all arguments are dimensionless (see sympy/physics/units/dimensions.py lines 397-416 and is_dimensionless at lines 447-455). The mismatch between get_dimensional_expr and _collect_factor_and_dimension is the reason exp(second/(ohm*farad)) ends up reported with the argument's Dimension rather than Dimension(1).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Calling UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension on expressions that include functions applied to dimensionless unit expressions should succeed and return a pair (scale_factor_expr, Dimension(1)) for the function result. For example, exp(second/(ohm*farad)) should be treated as dimensionless, and adding a dimensionless numeric literal (100) to it should not raise an exception.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension(100 + exp(second/(ohm*farad))) raises ValueError: Dimension of "exp(second/(farad*ohm))" is Dimension(time/(capacitance*impedance)), but it should be Dimension(1). The Function node was reported with the argument's Dimension rather than the function result's dimension.
