problem_statement.Title: parse_mathematica fails to parse Unicode identifiers (e.g. Greek 'λ')
problem_statement.Description: parse_mathematica raises a SyntaxError when given identifiers containing non-ASCII letters (for example 'λ'). The deprecated mathematica(...) path previously accepted such input; the parse_mathematica pipeline fails during tokenization/AST creation because the parser's regular expressions treat "letters" as ASCII-only.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) ASCII-only identifier regex in MathematicaParser._literal (sympy/parsing/mathematica.py): _literal is defined as "[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9]*" (line ~589) which requires the first character to be an ASCII letter and subsequent characters to be ASCII letters/digits. This excludes Unicode letters (e.g. Greek 'λ'). Correct relationship (Unicode-aware): identifiers must accept Unicode letters; one Unicode-aware pattern is "[^\W\d_][^\W_]*" (first char = Unicode letter, subsequent chars = Unicode letters/digits).

2) Multiple regexes assume "letter" == ASCII a-zA-Z (sympy/parsing/mathematica.py): several compiled patterns encode boundaries or letter tests using constructs like (?<=[a-zA-Z\d]), (?<=[a-zA-Z]), (?=[^a-zA-Z]) and character classes [a-zA-Z] / [^a-zA-Z]. Examples include RULES['whitespace'], RULES['add*_1'], RULES['add*_2'], RULES['Pi'], FM_PATTERN, and ARGS_PATTERN_TEMPLATE (lines ~176–180, 184–190, 192–199, 201–210, 213–220, 228–234). These treat letters as ASCII-only and therefore fail to recognize Unicode-letter boundaries. Correct relationship: use Unicode-aware classes for "letter" and non-letter boundaries (for example use [^\W\d_] to denote a Unicode letter and [^\w] or non-word lookarounds for non-letter boundaries); ensure regexes are treated with Unicode-aware semantics.

3) Tokenizer depends on the ASCII-only _literal (sympy/parsing/mathematica.py, MathematicaParser._get_tokenizer / _from_mathematica_to_tokens): the tokenizer alternation includes _literal; because _literal rejects Unicode letters, Unicode identifiers are not tokenized as single identifier tokens. This causes downstream parsing to fail and raises "unable to create a single AST for the expression." Correct relationship: after making identifier recognition Unicode-aware, the tokenizer should present Unicode identifier tokens as single tokens so later parsing stages produce a valid AST.

(Each of the above items is an independent incorrect assumption encoded in sympy/parsing/mathematica.py and all such occurrences must be treated equally: every explicit ASCII-letter class in the file that is used to identify letters or non-letters is part of the root cause.)
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python interpreter with SymPy (v1.11 or later).
Observe a SyntaxError: "unable to create a single AST for the expression"
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: parse_mathematica('λ') returns a SymPy symbol representing the identifier 'λ' (i.e. the parser accepts Unicode letters such as Greek characters as valid identifiers).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: parse_mathematica('λ') raises a SyntaxError: "unable to create a single AST for the expression" because the tokenizer/regexes do not recognize 'λ' as an identifier token.

problem_statement.Title: TensorProduct.expand(tensorproduct=True) fails to fully expand when factors include scalar (commutative) multiplicative factors
problem_statement.Description: Calling .expand(tensorproduct=True) on a TensorProduct whose factor(s) contain scalar (commutative) multiplicative parts can produce an incomplete expansion: the expansion stops because the code that decides whether to recurse into a constructed temporary assumes the temporary is a TensorProduct instance and misses the case where the constructor has pulled commutative scalars out front (returning a Mul). The symptom appears when a tensor factor is like (2*U - V), producing terms of the form Mul(scalar, TensorProduct(...)).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Wrong type-check assumption in the expand routine: sympy/physics/quantum/tensorproduct.py, class TensorProduct, method _eval_expand_tensorproduct. The method constructs tp = TensorProduct(... ) for subterms and only recurses when isinstance(tp, TensorProduct). This fails when the constructor returned a Mul wrapping a TensorProduct (e.g. Mul(scalar, TensorProduct(...))).

2) Constructor behavior that creates the situation: sympy/physics/quantum/tensorproduct.py, class TensorProduct, method __new__. __new__ intentionally pulls commutative (scalar) factors out and may return:
   - a plain commutative scalar when all parts are commutative (return c_part),
   - c_part * single_non_commutative when exactly one non-commutative factor remains (return Mul(*c_part) * new_args[0]), or
   - Mul(*c_part) * TensorProduct(*new_args) otherwise.
   Because of this, a freshly constructed tp can be a Mul(c_part, TensorProduct(...)) rather than a TensorProduct instance.

3) Correct semantic relationship that is not implemented by the current check: if a constructed tp equals Mul(*c_part, inner_tp) where inner_tp is a TensorProduct, expansion must behave as
   expanded = Mul(*c_part) * inner_tp._eval_expand_tensorproduct()
   and, if inner_tp._eval_expand_tensorproduct() returns an Add (a sum of terms), the scalar part must be distributed:
   Mul(*c_part) * Add(term_j)  =>  Add(Mul(*c_part)*term_j for each j).
   The current code does not perform this decomposition and distribution because it only tests isinstance(tp, TensorProduct) and otherwise appends tp unchanged.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: P.expand(tensorproduct=True) returns the fully distributed sum of tensor products: 2*TensorProduct(U, U) + 2*TensorProduct(U, V) - TensorProduct(V, U) - TensorProduct(V, V).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The expansion stops prematurely; for the example it yields: 2*TensorProduct(U, U + V) - TensorProduct(V, U + V) (i.e. the first factor was distributed but the second factor remained as the unreduced sum), instead of the fully expanded four-term sum.

problem_statement.Title: UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension rejects equivalent dimensions (acceleration*time vs velocity)
problem_statement.Description: When adding quantities whose dimensions are equivalent but represented with different symbolic structure (for example acceleration*time and velocity), UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension raises a ValueError instead of accepting the sum.
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) sympy/physics/units/unitsystem.py : UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension — In the Add branch the code enforces addend compatibility by structural equality of Dimension objects (the code checks `if dim != addend_dim:`; see unitsystem.py lines 173–184). This causes valid sums to be rejected when the two addends have dimensionally equivalent but differently-expressed Dimension objects (e.g., Dimension(acceleration*time) vs Dimension(velocity)).

2) sympy/physics/units/dimensions.py : DimensionSystem — The canonical notion of dimensional equivalence in this codebase is the equality of base-dimension dependency mappings computed by DimensionSystem.get_dimensional_dependencies and exposed via DimensionSystem.equivalent_dims (see dimensions.py lines 375–381 and 428–431). Two Dimension expressions that differ as symbolic expressions may nevertheless have identical dependency dicts (for example acceleration = length/time**2, so acceleration * time -> length/time which is the same dependency map as velocity). UnitSystem._collect_factor_and_dimension does not use this canonical equivalence and instead uses structural equality.

3) sympy/physics/units/systems/si.py : SI initialization block — the module contains a check that attempts to validate that declared scale factors match declared dimensions, but it calls `DimensionSystem.equivalent_dims` on the class rather than on the DimensionSystem instance (code: `if not DimensionSystem.equivalent_dims(_dimension, Dimension(dimex))` at si.py lines 336–339). equivalent_dims is implemented as an instance method that compares the dimension-system's canonical dependency maps; invoking it as shown is an incorrect usage and does not perform the intended instance-based equivalence check.
StepsToReproduce: Open a Python REPL with sympy installed.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Adding quantities whose dimensions are equivalent should succeed. In the example, adding a1*t1 and v1 should be allowed (they are both velocity in dimensional terms) and the call should return the combined numeric factor and a velocity dimension instead of raising.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: A ValueError is raised: "Dimension of \"v1\" is Dimension(velocity), but it should be Dimension(acceleration*time)" — the code treats Dimension(velocity) and Dimension(acceleration*time) as unequal (structurally different) and refuses the addition.

problem_statement.Title: Prefix × Unit incorrectly reduces to 1 (milli * W == 1)
problem_statement.Description: Multiplying a Prefix instance by a Quantity (unit) can return the plain number 1 when the product of their numeric scale_factors equals 1. Example: milli * W evaluates to 1 instead of a prefixed unit (milliwatt / mW).
problem_statement.RootCause:
1) Prefix.__mul__ in sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py computes fact = self.scale_factor * other.scale_factor and returns 1 if fact == 1 without first distinguishing whether 'other' is a Prefix or a Quantity. Because this early 'fact == 1' branch runs for Quantity operands, a prefix multiplied by certain units can collapse to the bare number 1. Location: sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py::Prefix.__mul__.

2) Prefix.__truediv__ in sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py uses the same early-check pattern: it computes fact = self.scale_factor / other.scale_factor and returns 1 if fact == 1 before distinguishing Prefix ÷ Prefix vs Prefix ÷ Quantity. This reproduces the same incorrect simplification on division. Location: sympy/physics/units/prefixes.py::Prefix.__truediv__.

3) Quantity.scale_factor (via UnitSystem.get_quantity_scale_factor) can be a numeric value derived from global registrations. In the repository's unit registrations, gram is used as the canonical mass unit and kilogram is registered via kilo relative to gram (kg = 1000 * g). That causes derived units built from kilogram (newton, joule, watt) to acquire numeric scale_factors (for example, watt.scale_factor == 1000). Because of (1) and (2) above, milli.scale_factor (1/1000) × watt.scale_factor (1000) == 1, which triggers the code to return the bare number 1 and lose unit information. Locations: sympy/physics/units/unit_definitions.py and the dimension/system files that register gram/kilogram (e.g., the length_weight_time / systems files used by SI).
StepsToReproduce: In Python with sympy physics units imported:
W * milli  # evaluates to watt*Prefix(milli, m, -3, 10) (inconsistent and incorrect behavior)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Multiplying a prefix by a unit should yield either the registered prefixed Quantity (if one exists) or the numeric factor times the Quantity, preserving unit information. Example: milli * W (or W * milli) should produce the prefixed unit milliwatt (a milliwatt Quantity or an expression equal to 1/1000 * watt), not the bare number 1.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: milli * W returns the plain number 1. W * milli returns a Product containing watt and a Prefix object, i.e., watt*Prefix(milli, ...). The two orders are inconsistent and the first loses unit information.
